```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant UI as NodeView (Editor Canvas)

    participant Button as CanvasRunWorkflowButton

    Note over User,API: Workflow Loading & Permission Evaluation

    UI->>Store: Fetch permissions & workflow metadata
    Store-->>UI: Return scopes (e.g., workflow:read, workflow:execute)

    alt NEW: Viewer Execution Mode
        UI->>UI: NEW: Set runWorkflowButtonLabel = "Start"
        opt Workflow has description
            UI->>User: NEW: Render N8nCallout (Viewer Manual instructions)
        end
    else Standard Editor Mode
        UI->>UI: Set runWorkflowButtonLabel = "Execute workflow"
    end

    UI->>Button: Render with label and execution permissions

    Note over User,API: Execution Flow

    User->>Button: Click "Start"
    
    Button->>API: CHANGED: POST /execute (Now allowed for Viewer role)
    
    alt Execution Success
        API-->>Button: Return executionId
        Button->>UI: Transition to "Executing" state
        UI->>User: Update UI with execution progress
    else Execution Error
        API-->>Button: 403 Forbidden / 500 Error
        Button->>User: Show workflow error toast
    end
```
</details>

body:
Large viewer-mode state and UI were added inside NodeView.vue (computed properties, viewer form handling, file conversion, template markup). Consider extracting into a dedicated component/composable to avoid mixing viewer logic with canvas responsibilities.

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​A large amount of viewer-mode state, computed properties, file conversion helpers, form-handling functions, and template markup were added to the NodeView component. This mixes a new feature's data handling and UI with the already complex canvas/editor responsibilities, increasing cognitive load and making the file harder to navigate, test, and maintain. Extracting the viewer inputs logic and UI into a dedicated composable and/or child component would keep responsibilities separated and the NodeView focused on canvas lifecycle and node operations.

**:wrench: How do I fix it?**
Split large files into smaller, focused modules. Each file should have a single responsibility.

body:
Introduced large viewer-mode input & execution logic into NodeView.vue; consider moving form handling and file conversion into a dedicated composable or child component to avoid bloating the already large component.

<details>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​The changes add an entire viewer execution feature — imports, computed properties, reactive state, file handling (convertFileToBinaryData), input sanitization, form state management, UI markup, and execution handling — directly into NodeView.vue. Such additions make the file harder to navigate and maintain, and would be better organized into smaller, focused composables or child components.

body:
Inline watch callback contains multi-line logic; extract into a descriptively-named function to improve readability and testability.

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​A multi-line inline arrow callback was added as the second argument to a watch call. This callback contains non-trivial logic: building new values, iterating fields, initializing nextValues/nextFiles, and assigning reactive refs. Because it performs substantial business logic (sanitizing and resetting form state) placing it inline hides the operation's intent and makes it harder to test or reuse. Extracting it to a named function with a descriptive name would improve readability and testability.

**:wrench: How do I fix it?**
Extract complex anonymous functions into named functions with descriptive names, or add explanatory comments for their purpose.

Adds several Viewer* types and viewerMode to IWorkflowSettings inside a very large interfaces file; consider isolating viewer-related types into a separate module for clarity and maintainability.

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​The change introduces several new Viewer-related type definitions and a new settings property into an already very large, general-purpose interfaces file. This increases the surface area and mixes viewer-specific configuration with many other unrelated interfaces and domains. Such additions make it harder to find, review, and maintain viewer-specific types because they are buried inside a monolithic file that spans many responsibilities. Keeping feature-specific types closer to the feature (or grouped into a dedicated module) would improve discoverability and limit future merge conflicts. This finding only concerns the newly added viewer types and the viewerMode property that were introduced in the diff.

**:wrench: How do I fix it?**
Split large files into smaller, focused modules. Each file should have a single responsibility.

body:
Anonymous watcher callback contains multi-line state mapping logic; extract into a named function for clarity and testability

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​An anonymous callback passed directly to a reactive watcher was added. This callback contains multi-step transformation logic: it creates new objects, iterates fields, sets up default values for different field types, and replaces reactive state. At ~25+ lines it is non-trivial. Complex anonymous callbacks reduce readability and make unit testing and reuse harder. Extracting this logic into a named helper improves discoverability and allows direct testing.

What is this code trying to accomplish? It synchronizes viewer input definitions with form state when viewerInputFields change.
Does this actually harm maintainability? Yes — the multi-line anonymous watcher body hides business logic inside a reactive hook. Naming the function clarifies intent and facilitates testing.
Is it appropriate here? The watcher should delegate to a small named function. Fix is small: extract the callback to a named function and pass that to watch.
Is it fixable within PR scope? Yes — move the body into a named function defined near related state, and use that function as the watcher callback.

**:wrench: How do I fix it?**
Extract complex anonymous functions into named functions with descriptive names, or add explanatory comments for their purpose.

<sub>Reply `@AikidoSec feedback: [FEEDBACK]` to get better review comments in the future.</sub>
<sub>Reply `@AikidoSec ignore: [REASON]` to ignore this issue.</sub>
<sub>[More info](https://app.aikido.dev/repositories/306852/pull_requests/26720/latest?groupId=1590&issue_hash=eyJ0eXBlIjoiY29kZV9xdWFsaXR5IiwicnVsZV9pZCI6IkFJS19BSV9leGNlc3NfYW5vbnltb3VzX2Z1bmN0aW9ucyIsImZpbGUiOiJwYWNrYWdlc1wvZnJvbnRlbmRcL2VkaXRvci11aVwvc3JjXC9hcHBcL3ZpZXdzXC9Ob2RlVmlldy52dWUiLCJzdGFydF9saW5lIjozNjZ9)</sub>
</details>

<file name="packages/@n8n/permissions/src/roles/scopes/project-scopes.ee.ts">

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/permissions/src/roles/scopes/project-scopes.ee.ts:135">
P1: Custom agent: **Security Review**

Adding `workflow:execute` to `PROJECT_VIEWER_SCOPES` is a significant privilege escalation. The Viewer role is intended to be read-only, and this scope grants the ability to execute **any** workflow in the project — not just form-based ones. The more restrictive `workflow:execute-chat` was previously the only execution-related scope for viewers (and is the only scope for `PROJECT_CHAT_USER_SCOPES`). Consider introducing a more granular scope (e.g., `workflow:execute-form`) or adding runtime checks to limit execution to form-triggered workflows only.</violation>
</file>

<file name="packages/frontend/editor-ui/src/app/views/NodeView.vue">

<violation number="1" location="packages/frontend/editor-ui/src/app/views/NodeView.vue:336">
P1: Custom agent: **Use workflowDocumentStore for Migrated Workflow Fields**

`settings` is a migrated field — read it from `workflowDocumentStore` instead of `workflowsStore.workflow.settings`. The component already injects `workflowDocumentStore` and uses it for other migrated fields like `active` and `pinData`.</violation>
</file>
```

</details>

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User (Viewer/Editor)

        WS->>API: Save Workflow (settings.viewerMode)
    end

    U->>NV: Open Workflow
    NV->>NV: NEW: Check isViewerExecutionMode

    alt Form Defined
        NV->>NV: NEW: Render Viewer Inputs Panel
        U->>NV: Input data / Upload files
        U->>NV: Click "Start"
        NV->>NV: NEW: getMissingViewerInputLabels() (Validation)
        NV->>NV: NEW: createViewerNodeData() (JSON + Binary)
    else No Form
        U->>NV: Click "Execute workflow"
    end

    NV->>RW: CHANGED: runEntireWorkflow(source, trigger, nodeData)
    RW->>API: NEW: POST /execute with injected input data
    API-->>RW: Execution Started
    RW-->>NV: Update UI State

    Note over U, API: Error Handling
    alt Invalid Schema/Input
        WS-->>U: NEW: Error: "Invalid Schema"
        NV-->>U: NEW: Error: "Please complete required fields"

body:
Global runtime patch of child_process (patchChildProcessGlobally) intercepts spawn/exec/etc., which can hide or alter subprocess behavior and environment; review for privacy/security implications.

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​The change introduces a global patch of Node's child_process module at TaskRunner construction time. It replaces spawn/exec/execFile/fork with wrappers that mutate options.env and add logic to detect specific commands (claude) and set environment variables (DISABLE_PROMPT_CACHING). Globally overriding built-in APIs changes runtime behavior for all code running in the process and for any dynamic code executed later; such interception is a common technique used to hide or alter subprocess execution and to silently strip or inject environment data. This can obscure what subprocesses are actually run, their effective environment, and side effects, making auditing and threat detection harder. The patch is located inside JsTaskRunner.patchChildProcessGlobally() and is applied when mode === 'secure'.

**:wrench: How do I fix it?**
Ensure code is transparent and not intentionally obfuscated. Avoid hiding functionality from code review. Focus on intent and deception, not specific patterns.

body:
**10 issues found** across 10 files
Prompt for AI agents (unresolved issues) ```text Check if these issues are valid — if so, understand the root cause of each and fix them. If appropriate, use sub-agents to investigate and fix each issue separately. P2: Build steps use mutable 'latest' tag for kaniko executor, reducing build reproducibility and increasing supply-chain risk. Consider pinning to a specific version with digest (e.g., executor:v1.x.x@sha256:...). P1: Remote installer scripts and binaries are downloaded and executed without checksum or signature verification, creating a supply-chain compromise path. The trivy install script is piped directly to shell, and gitleaks/gosec binaries are downloaded without integrity checks. This is inconsistent with the secure pattern used elsewhere in this codebase where .sha256 files are downloaded alongside binaries. P2: Missing `N8N_RUNNERS_GRACEFUL_SHUTDOWN_TIMEOUT` from runner env allowlists: The new env var is configured in the codebase but is not added to the `allowed-env` lists in `docker/images/runners/n8n-task-runners.json`. Both JavaScript (lines 9-35) and Python (lines 48-59) runner configurations need to include this variable so it propagates from parent to runner process when running in external/Docker mode. Without this, operators can set the environment variable but runners will use the default value of 10 seconds instead. Add `N8N_RUNNERS_GRACEFUL_SHUTDOWN_TIMEOUT` to both allowlists alongside similar variables like `N8N_RUNNERS_AUTO_SHUTDOWN_TIMEOUT`. P2: Test duplicates `createSignalHandler` logic instead of exercising production code, so regressions in start.ts timeout wiring may go undetected. The test implementation differs from production in critical ways: module-level vs per-handler `isShuttingDown` state, timing of state changes, and complete absence of cleanup verification. Consider exporting the production function for testing or restructuring to test through the production integration point. P1: child_process wrapper breaks standard overloaded signatures (spawn/exec/execFile/fork). The wrapper uses fixed positional destructuring that doesn't handle Node.js's overloaded API signatures, causing callback loss or invalid argument forwarding. Examples: `spawn('cmd', { cwd: '/tmp' })` passes options object as args; `exec('cmd', callback)` loses the callback; `execFile('file', callback)` passes callback as args. Fixed parameter definitions conflict with Node's internal normalization functions (normalizeSpawnArguments, normalizeExecArgs, etc.). P1: Custom agent: **Tests** No tests were added for the security-critical `createSecureChildProcessWrapper` function. At minimum, tests should verify that wrapped methods strip sensitive env vars and that sync variants are also covered. P0: Custom agent: **Security Review** The sync methods `execSync`, `spawnSync`, and `execFileSync` are passed through unwrapped via the `...childProcess` spread, allowing sandbox code to bypass the env-var stripping entirely (e.g. `require('child_process').execSync('env')` leaks all sensitive tokens). Wrap these three methods the same way the async variants are wrapped. P0: Custom agent: **Security Review** Whitelisting `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in `allowed-env` exposes this credential to the task runner process. Combined with `child_process` being added to allowed builtins (same diff), any Code node user can trivially extract this API key. This violates the rule's criteria on credential exposure and `process.env` access in Code nodes. API keys should be managed through n8n's credential system rather than passed as environment variables accessible to user code. P0: Custom agent: **Security Review** Adding `child_process` and `fs` to `NODE_FUNCTION_ALLOW_BUILTIN` is a critical sandbox weakening. Code node users can execute arbitrary system commands via `child_process` (e.g., `require('child_process').execSync('env')`) and read/write arbitrary files via `fs`. This effectively removes sandbox isolation for the JS task runner. This violates the rule's criteria on "New access to Node.js builtins or external modules in sandboxed contexts" and "Modifications to Code node sandboxes that reduce isolation." If this is intentional for a private deployment, it should not be merged into the shared configuration without explicit documentation of the security trade-offs and who has access to create Code node workflows. P0: Custom agent: **Security Review** **Security: credential stripping bypass via Node.js argument overloading.** `spawn`, `execFile`, and `fork` all accept an optional `args` array as the second parameter. When called without `args` (e.g. `spawn('cmd', { env: process.env })`), the options object lands in the `args` slot, `options` is `undefined`, and the secure env replacement is placed in a third argument that Node.js ignores. The auth tokens (`N8N_RUNNERS_GRANT_TOKEN`, `N8N_RUNNERS_AUTH_TOKEN`) pass through unstripped. The patch must detect whether the second argument is an array (args) or a plain object (options), mirroring Node's own `normalizeSpawnArguments` behavior. The same fix is needed for `execFile` and `fork` below. ```
Architecture diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Orchestrator as Container Orchestrator participant OS as OS / Process participant Start as Runner (start.ts) participant Config as BaseRunnerConfig participant JS as JsTaskRunner participant CP as child_process (Patched) participant Task as In-flight Task Note over Start,Config: Initialization Phase Start->>Config: Initialize Config->>Config: NEW: Read N8N_RUNNERS_GRACEFUL_SHUTDOWN_TIMEOUT Config-->>Start: Config loaded (default 10s) Start->>JS: new JsTaskRunner() JS->>CP: NEW: patchChildProcessGlobally() Note right of CP: Overwrites spawn/exec to strip
N8N_RUNNERS_AUTH_TOKEN & API Keys Start->>OS: Register SIGINT/SIGTERM handlers Note over Start,OS: CHANGED: Handlers now use
Config.gracefulShutdownTimeout Note over Orchestrator,Task: Runtime Execution & Shutdown Flow Task->>CP: User code calls spawn/exec CP->>CP: NEW: stripSensitiveEnvVars() CP->>OS: Execute subprocess (Isolated env) Orchestrator->>OS: SIGTERM (Scale down) OS->>Start: Trigger Signal Handler Start->>Start: NEW: Set force-shutdown timer (Config.gracefulShutdownTimeout) alt Happy Path: Tasks complete before timeout Task-->>Start: Task finished Start->>OS: process.exit(0) else Unhappy Path: Timeout reached Start->>OS: NEW: process.exit(1) (Forced) Note over Start,OS: Prevents runner from hanging
and blocking orchestrator end Note over JS,CP: Module Resolution Flow Task->>JS: require('child_process') JS->>JS: NEW: createSecureChildProcessWrapper() JS-->>Task: Return wrapped child_process ```
Reply with feedback, questions, or to request a fix. Tag `@cubic-dev-ai` to re-run a review.

Code node users can execute arbitrary system commands via `child_process` (e.g., `require('child_process').execSync('env')`) and read/write arbitrary files via `fs`. This violates the rule's criteria on "New access to Node.js builtins or external modules in sandboxed contexts" and "Modifications to Code node sandboxes that reduce isolation." P1: child_process wrapper breaks standard overloaded signatures (spawn/exec/execFile/fork). body:
The sms:send handling starting at the options retrieval is deeply nested (WhatsApp/template/text/media branches). Prefer early validation/guard clauses or early continue to flatten the for-loop logic and make the main message-building path clearer.

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​The execute loop's sms:send branch now contains a large multi-level nested structure around WhatsApp/template handling and message/media validation. This buries validation and error handling inside deep branches; using early guard checks or early continue after simple validations (e.g., invalid combinations, missing required fields) would flatten the main path and improve readability and maintainability.

**:wrench: How do I fix it?**
Place parameter validation and guard clauses at the function start. Use early returns to reduce nesting levels and improve readability.

body:
In call:make, body.StatusCallback is assigned from options.statusCallback and then immediately overwritten by getNodeParameter('options.statusCallback'), so the new options-based logic can never take effect.

```suggestion
						body.StatusCallback = (options.statusCallback as string) || '';
```

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​The new logic sets a value based on the newly introduced "options" collection, but right after that the code assigns the same field again from a different parameter path. Because both assignments always execute in sequence, the earlier assignment can never influence the request payload, making the new branch ineffective and producing behavior inconsistent with the introduced option.

body:
findPackageVersion introduces filesystem/package.json discovery into the same module that implements the LmChatAnthropic node; separate package-metadata lookup from the node implementation.

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​A new package-version discovery function (reads package.json via fs and walks parent directories) was added to the same module that implements the LmChatAnthropic node. This mixes build-time metadata retrieval (filesystem I/O) with runtime LLM client construction and networking concerns. Combining package-file discovery logic with the node's networking/LLM responsibilities increases coupling and spreads unrelated responsibilities across the same component, making testing and maintenance harder.

**:wrench: How do I fix it?**
Split classes that handle database, HTTP, and UI concerns into separate, focused classes.

<details>
<summary>Architecture diagram</summary>

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Card as SecretsProviderConnectionCard
    participant View as SettingsSecretsProviders
    participant Comp as useSecretsProviderConnection
    participant API as RestApiClient
    participant BE as Backend API
    participant Toast as Toast UI

    Note over User,BE: NEW: Admin Activation of Inactive Secret Store

    User->>Card: View Connection List
    Card->>Card: Check provider.isEnabled === false
    opt isEnabled is false
        Card->>Card: NEW: Show "Inactive" Badge
        Card->>Card: CHANGED: Hide "Reload" action from menu
        alt NEW: Has 'externalSecretsProvider:update' permission
            Card->>Card: NEW: Render Activation Toggle
        end
    end

    User->>Card: Click Activation Toggle
    Card->>View: emit toggleEnabled(providerKey)
    View->>View: NEW: Set activatingProvider = providerKey

    View->>Comp: activateConnection(providerKey)
    Comp->>API: NEW: enableSecretProviderConnection(key)
    API->>BE: PATCH /secret-providers/connections/:key { isEnabled: true }

    alt Success Path
        BE-->>API: 200 OK (Updated Connection)
        API-->>View: Return Connection
        View->>Toast: NEW: showMessage("Secret store active")
        View->>View: fetchConnection(providerKey) to refresh state
    else Error Path

addItemToFixedCollection now contains multiple branches and fallback locator strategies, increasing complexity in a large page-object class; consider extracting this logic into a small helper module or utility to reduce the burden on the page class.

<details>

body:
The command 'npm rebuild isolated-vm 2>/dev/null || true' suppresses errors and forces success; remove stderr redirection and '|| true' so rebuild failures surface during builds.

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​A shell command was modified to redirect stderr to /dev/null and then use '|| true' to force success. This construct suppresses errors and makes the command always succeed, which hides runtime failures and diagnostic output. Hiding errors like this reduces transparency during builds and runtime, making debugging and auditing harder and can mask problematic behavior. The rest of the changes simply add an additional native-rebuild invocation without similar suppression, so the introduced suppression is the new risk. This is a targeted, small-scope issue that can be fixed in the PR by removing stderr redirection and the unconditional success fallback or by handling failures explicitly.

**:wrench: How do I fix it?**
Ensure code is transparent and not intentionally obfuscated. Avoid hiding functionality from code review. Focus on intent and deception, not specific patterns.

<sub>Reply `@AikidoSec feedback: [FEEDBACK]` to get better review comments in the future.</sub>
<sub>Reply `@AikidoSec ignore: [REASON]` to ignore this issue.</sub>

body:
The javascript-runner-builder stage uses node:*-bookworm-slim (Debian), but the new RUN step calls `apk`, which cannot exist there, so the rebuild step will always fail.

<details>
<summary>Show fix</summary>

```suggestion
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends python3 make g++ && \
    npm rebuild isolated-vm && \
    apt-get purge -y python3 make g++ && apt-get autoremove -y && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
```

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​The added rebuild step assumes the presence of the `apk` package manager to install build dependencies. However, this build stage is based on a Debian image, where `apk` is not available. As a result, the command will fail immediately, preventing the image from building and making the intended rebuild of `isolated-vm` impossible in this stage.

<sub>Reply `@AikidoSec feedback: [FEEDBACK]` to get better review comments in the future.</sub>

body:
Empty catch silently swallows errors when fetching workflow version. Add logging or explicit handling (e.g., telemetry or a no-op fallback) so missing or failed version fetches are observable.

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​A new catch block was added to the async watcher that fetches a workflow version. The catch only contains a comment and no logging, error propagation, or handling. This silently swallows any error from workflowHistoryStore.getWorkflowVersion, which can hide runtime failures and make debugging harder. Even though the comment states the version may be pruned and the error is being ignored, there is no observable handling (e.g., telemetry or harmless fallback) to record or clarify this behavior at runtime. Adding minimal handling would improve observability and maintainability.

**:wrench: How do I fix it?**
Add proper error handling in catch blocks. Log the error, show user feedback, or rethrow if needed.

<sub>Reply `@AikidoSec feedback: [FEEDBACK]` to get better review comments in the future.</sub>

body:
unsetCredential may perform node iteration and credential computation even when workflowDocumentStore?.value is null; add an early guard to return if workflowDocumentStore?.value is absent.

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​unsetCredential now calls workflowDocumentStore?.value?.updateNodeProperties which may be a no-op if the injected store is missing. The function still iterates and computes updates, leading to wasted computation and no persisted changes. An early return when workflowDocumentStore?.value is absent would make behavior explicit and avoid unnecessary work.

**:wrench: How do I fix it?**
Place parameter validation and guard clauses at the function start. Use early returns to reduce nesting levels and improve readability.

body:
**No issues found** across 2 files

<details>
<summary>Architecture diagram</summary>

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Node as Workflow Node Execution
    participant Helpers as Request Helper Functions
    participant SSRF as SSRF Protection Service
    participant DNS as DNS Resolver
    participant Axios as HTTP Client (Axios)
    participant Target as External Server

    Note over Node,Target: SSRF Guarded Request Flow

    Node->>Helpers: httpRequest(options)
    
    alt SSRF Bridge present
        Helpers->>SSRF: NEW: validateUrl(targetUrl)
        SSRF->>DNS: NEW: lookup(hostname)
        DNS-->>SSRF: IP Address(es)
        
        alt IP/Host Blocked (Private IP, not in allowlist)
            SSRF-->>Helpers: Throw UserError
            Helpers-->>Node: Error: IP address is blocked
        else IP/Host Allowed (Public or Allowlisted)
            SSRF-->>Helpers: Validation Success
            
            Helpers->>Axios: NEW: request() with secureLookup injected
            Axios->>Target: Network Request
            
            alt Server returns Redirect (3xx)
                Target-->>Axios: Redirect Location
                Axios->>SSRF: NEW: validateRedirectSync(location)
                
                alt Redirect Target Blocked
                    SSRF-->>Axios: Throw UserError
                    Axios-->>Helpers: Request Failed
                else Redirect Target Allowed
                    Axios->>Target: Follow Redirect
                    Target-->>Axios: Response
                end
            else Standard Response (2xx)
                Target-->>Axios: Response Data
            end
            
            Axios-->>Helpers: Result
            Helpers-->>Node: Data
        end
    else SSRF Bridge absent (System-level calls)
        Helpers->>Axios: Direct request (unguarded)
        Axios->>Target: Network Request
        Target-->>Axios: Response
        Axios-->>Helpers: Result
        Helpers-->>Node: Data
    end

    Note over Helpers,SSRF: NEW: Integration tests verify combined logic of DNS + Allowlist + Redirects
```
</details>

body:
execute method nests a for-loop inside a try with an inner try/catch that wraps the entire per-item logic. Use early-continue/guard clauses to flatten the loop and reduce nesting (e.g., validate/skip invalid items before the main work).

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​The execute method contains an outer try/finally that encompasses a for-loop, and each loop iteration wraps nearly all per-item logic inside a nested try/catch. That 3-level nesting buries the main flow and makes it harder to follow. Using early continues/guard clauses for invalid/short-circuit cases would flatten the loop and improve readability while preserving the existing per-item error handling.

**:wrench: How do I fix it?**
Place parameter validation and guard clauses at the function start. Use early returns to reduce nesting levels and improve readability.

<sub>Reply `@AikidoSec feedback: [FEEDBACK]` to get better review comments in the future.</sub>
<sub>Reply `@AikidoSec ignore: [REASON]` to ignore this issue.</sub>

(Based on your team's feedback about guarding long-lived async waits in CRDT transport tests.) @@ -1,5 +1,21 @@ import { MessagePortTransport } from './message-port'; +function waitForMessage(transport: MessagePortTransport): { + promise: Promise; + received: Uint8Array[]; ```
```suggestion function waitForMessage( transport: MessagePortTransport, timeoutMs = 1000, ): { promise: Promise; received: Uint8Array[]; } { const received: Uint8Array[] = []; let timeout: ReturnType | undefined; let unsubscribe = () => {}; const promise = new Promise((resolve, reject) => { timeout = setTimeout(() => { unsubscribe(); reject(new Error(`Timed out waiting for message after ${timeoutMs}ms`)); }, timeoutMs); unsubscribe = transport.onReceive((data) => { received.push(data); if (timeout) clearTimeout(timeout); unsubscribe(); resolve(data); }); }); return { promise, received }; } ```

body: Good point. Actually, removing files while indexing can lead to orphaned documents in the vector store and broken search result. I'd rather disallow to remove files until they become indexed or error. User can always delete the agent itself if indexing never ends for whatever reason.

author: geemanjs

body:
This is a well-structured feature addition. The `#discoverKvMounts` abstraction cleanly separates manual vs auto-discovery paths, and the test coverage is comprehensive.

A few specific observations:

1. **`#normalizeKvPath` trailing slash** — Good defensive handling. Worth noting that this also handles the case where a user passes `secret` without a trailing slash, which is tested. However, if a user passes something like `secret//` (double trailing slash), the current logic wouldn't normalize that. Probably an edge case not worth handling, but a `trimEnd('/')` + append `/` pattern would be more robust.

2. **`kvVersion` default fallback** — In `#discoverKvMounts`, when `kvMountPath` is set, you fall back to `kvVersion ?? '2'`. The UI default is also `'2'`. This is consistent, but the `kvVersion` field in the UI has `required: false` — consider whether this should be `required: true` with a `displayOptions.show` conditional on `kvMountPath` being non-empty, so the user is prompted to choose a version when they specify a path.

3. **Error message consistency** — In the original code, the sys/mounts failure message was `"Couldn't list mounts but wasn't a permissions issue."` (missing "it"). The new code says `"Couldn't list mounts but it wasn't a permissions issue."` — good, this fixes a minor grammar issue.

4. **`#testSecretAccess` for KV v1** — For v1, the list URL is `${normalizedPath}?list=true` without the `metadata/` segment. This correctly mirrors the `update()` flow. The test for v1 validation (`should validate KV v1 path without metadata segment`) confirms this path works.

5. **Test helpers** — `vaultSettingsWithKvPath` and `tokenLookupResponse` are nice test utilities. Consider whether `tokenLookupResponse` could replace the inline token setup in the existing tests above for consistency.

Solid work — the feature enables a meaningful use case (restricted Vault tokens without `sys/mounts` access) with clear code and good test coverage.

body:
Nice additions — the `findEmail` operation and credential testing are useful features. A few things I noticed:

1. **`findEmail` error handling** — In `CustomerIo.node.ts`, `responseData = responseData.results` assumes the API always returns a `results` property. If the API returns an error or an unexpected shape, this would silently set `responseData` to `undefined`. Consider adding a guard: `responseData = responseData?.results ?? [];`

2. **Removed beta API hostnames** — In `CustomerIoApi.credentials.ts`, you removed `beta-api.customer.io` and `beta-api-eu.customer.io` from the hostname check. If any existing users had configured beta API endpoints, their requests would no longer get the `Bearer` auth header. Was this intentional? If Customer.io has deprecated the beta endpoints, a note in the PR description would help reviewers understand the rationale.

3. **`_query` → `query` in GenericFunctions.ts** — Good fix. The unused parameter prefixed with `_` was effectively a bug since callers could pass query params that were silently ignored. The `findEmail` operation depends on this fix (it passes `{ email }` as query), so this is a prerequisite change.

4. **Credential test: partial validation** — The `testCustomerIoApi` method validates the App API key only if provided (`if (hasAppKey)`). This means users can pass credential tests without an App API key, but then the `findEmail` operation would fail at runtime since it uses the `app` base API. Consider either:
   - Making the App API key required when `findEmail` is used, or
   - Adding a note in the `findEmail` field description that the App API key must be configured

5. **Test coverage** — The `nock`-based tests and workflow JSON fixtures are well-structured. Good use of `NodeTestHarness` for the integration tests.

Extracting operation-specific logic into smaller helper functions or using early-continue/return guards per operation would flatten the control flow and improve readability and maintainability.

Place parameter validation and guard clauses at the function start. Use early returns to reduce nesting levels and improve readability.

		`Determined track info: track=${track}, version=${packageVersion}, new_stable_version=${newStable}, release_type=${releaseType}, rc_branch=${rc_branch}`,
```

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​The console.log call was modified to include previousVersion in the string logged to stdout. This increases ad-hoc runtime logging produced by the script. Because console.log is used in a CI utility script, increasing the data logged can leak internal state or produce noisy logs. The change is an introduced/worsened logging artifact in this PR.

body:
hasNodeEnhancements uses /(fix|feat)\((.*Node|nodes)\)/; the '.*' inside alternation can lead to catastrophic backtracking on long inputs. Restrict or avoid unbounded '.*' in that alternation.

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​The hasNodeEnhancements function uses a regex with an alternation containing '.*' (match-all) followed by a literal. When applied to potentially large or crafted input, the '.*' can consume and then backtrack repeatedly to try to satisfy the following literal, causing heavy CPU usage. This change introduced that pattern where previously no such vulnerable pattern existed.

**:wrench: How do I fix it?**
Avoid nested quantifiers like (x+)+ and ambiguous patterns. Use atomic groups, possessive quantifiers, or rewrite complex regex patterns as simpler alternatives.

<sub>Reply `@AikidoSec feedback: [FEEDBACK]` to get better review comments in the future.</sub>

<details>
<summary>Architecture diagram</summary>

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Process as CLI / Server Process
    participant AE as ActiveExecutions Service
    participant Map as CHANGED: Map (activeExecutions)
    participant WE as Workflow Execution instance

    Note over AE, Map: Execution Lifecycle Management

    Process->>AE: add(executionId, data)
    AE->>Map: CHANGED: get(executionId)
    Map-->>AE: existing record (if resuming)
    AE->>Map: CHANGED: set(executionId, execution)
    
    opt Streaming Result
        Process->>AE: sendChunk(executionId, chunk)
        AE->>Map: CHANGED: get(executionId)
        Map-->>AE: execution.httpResponse
        AE->>Process: write(chunk)
    end

    Note over AE, WE: Completion Flow (Deferred Promise)
    
    WE-->>AE: postExecutePromise resolves
    alt Still Resuming
        AE->>AE: Remove workflowExecution reference
    else Fully Finished
        AE->>Map: CHANGED: delete(executionId)
        AE->>AE: Log "Execution removed"
    end

    Note over AE, Map: Monitoring & Maintenance

    Process->>AE: getActiveExecutions()
    AE->>Map: CHANGED: direct iteration (for...of Map)
    Map-->>AE: id, data
    AE-->>Process: return IExecutionsCurrentSummary[]

    Note over AE, Map: Shutdown Sequence

    Process->>AE: shutdown()
    AE->>Map: CHANGED: iterate keys/values
    loop for each execution
        alt Should Cancel
            AE->>AE: stopExecution(id)
        else Is Pending
            AE->>Map: CHANGED: delete(id)
        end
    end
    
    loop Until Map is Empty
        AE->>Map: CHANGED: check .size property
        AE->>Process: sleep(500)
    end
```
</details>

body:
Nice refactor — `Map` is the right data structure here and eliminates the prototype chain concerns of plain objects.

A couple of observations:

1. **Deleting from a Map during iteration** — In `shutdown()`, you iterate with `for (const [executionId, { status }] of this.activeExecutions)` and then call `this.activeExecutions.delete(executionId)` inside the loop body (via `stopExecution` or directly). While ES6 Maps do support safe deletion during `for...of` iteration (per spec), this relies on a subtlety that can surprise future contributors. A brief comment would help, or you could collect IDs to delete and batch them after the loop, similar to how `toCancel` is already used for the concurrency control removal.

2. **`getActiveExecutions()` improvement** — Since you're already using `Map`, you could simplify the loop with destructuring: the current code uses `for (const [id, data] of this.activeExecutions)` which is already clean, nice.

3. **Minor: the shutdown polling loop** — The `while (this.activeExecutions.size !== 0)` is cleaner than the old `Object.keys` approach since it avoids re-creating an array each iteration. Good improvement.

Overall this is a clean, straightforward migration. The behavioral semantics are preserved correctly. 👍

    participant FS as File System (FileHandle)
    participant Zlib as Node.js zlib (Inflate)

    Note over CLI,Zlib: NEW: Central Directory-first Decompression Flow

    CLI->>Util: decompressFolder(sourcePath, outputDir)
    Util->>FS: open(sourcePath, 'r')
    FS-->>Util: fileHandle

    Note over Util,FS: Metadata Discovery (Handles ZIP64)
    Util->>FS: NEW: readEOCD (Search end of file)
    FS-->>Util: EOCD / EOCD64 record
    Util->>FS: NEW: readCentralDirectory(cdOffset, cdSize)
    FS-->>Util: List of ZipEntries (Offsets/Sizes)

    loop For each ZipEntry
        Util->>Util: Sanitize path
        
        alt Path is source ZIP (Self-inclusion)
            Util->>Util: NEW: Skip entry (Prevents truncation)
        else Path is valid file
            Util->>FS: NEW: getDataOffset(localHeaderOffset)
            FS-->>Util: dataStartOffset
            
            Util->>FS: NEW: readFileChunks(dataStartOffset, compressedSize)
            
            Note over Util,Zlib: Constant memory streaming
            Util->>Zlib: CHANGED: pipeline(readStream, createInflateRaw(), writeStream)
            Zlib->>FS: Write decompressed file to outputDir
        end
    end

    Util->>FS: close()
    Util-->>CLI: Decompression Complete
```
</details>

body:
Promise.catch on mkdir swallowed EEXIST silently; log or explicitly handle the EEXIST case instead of silently ignoring it so directory-creation errors aren't hidden.

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​A Promise catch attached to mkdir swallowed the EEXIST case silently while rejecting other errors. Silently ignoring EEXIST can hide race conditions or unexpected states during extraction and makes failures harder to diagnose. The removed handler changed how directory creation errors were surfaced, altering error-handling behavior.

**:wrench: How do I fix it?**
Add proper error handling in catch blocks. Log the error, show user feedback, or rethrow if needed.

            Zlib->>Out: Write decompressed data
        else compressionMethod == STORED
            Utils->>Out: pipeline(readStream, writeStream)
        end
    end

author: CharlieKolb

body:
**21 issues found** across 36 files
Prompt for AI agents (unresolved issues) ```text Check if these issues are valid — if so, understand the root cause of each and fix them. If appropriate, use sub-agents to investigate and fix each issue separately. P1: Manual-mode model changes are not persisted here; this call only saves the category, so the selected model is lost after reload. P2: These selectors are nested under `.modelBar`, but the template renders `modelBarRow` as the wrapper and `modelBarPercent` as a sibling, so the flex row layout never applies. P2: When AI Gateway is enabled, the Model dropdown still loads from the Google `/v1beta/models` endpoint via `$credentials.host`, so model selection will fail or show the wrong catalog for `n8nAiGatewayApi` users. P1: This mock drops `getNodeParameter` fallback values, so the category-resolution tests don't exercise the real code path. P2: This assertion is inverted: the node description does include a `model` property for manual mode. P2: Guard this route behind the AI Gateway feature flag; otherwise users can open a settings page that silently falls back when the backend module is disabled. P1: Custom agent: **Security Review** This settings route is missing RBAC for an instance-wide feature. With only `authenticated` here, any signed-in user can open AI Gateway settings, read global usage, and change the default gateway category for the whole instance. P2: This credential is already registered earlier in the array, so this addition creates a duplicate entry. P2: The new Scenario 1 example maps `Balanced` to the wrong model; earlier in the same spec `Balanced` is defined as `openai/gpt-4.1-mini`, not `openai/gpt-4.1-nano`. P3: This introduces a duplicate `Scenario 2` heading, which makes the workflow scenarios ambiguous in the rendered spec. P1: A transient AI Gateway fetch failure becomes permanent because the store is marked initialized anyway. P2: This line says `resolveProviderKey()` was removed, but the store still defines and uses it for provider metadata. P1: Treat empty model IDs as unset here; the credential default `''` can bypass the fallback and send `model: ''`. P2: Changing the category here clobbers the workflow's manual model override, so toggling away from Manual and back can silently save a different execution model. P2: Gate this menu item behind an actual AI Gateway availability flag. Right now any authenticated user can see it, even when the backend feature is off, and the page degrades into silent fallback data. P2: Add a supplyData test for the AI Gateway credential path. The new credential is only asserted in the node description, so regressions in the `n8nAiGatewayApi`/`ChatOpenAI` branch would currently go unnoticed. P1: `manual` is a supported gateway category, but this validation coerces it to `balanced`, so the provisioned credential ignores the user's manual-mode setting. P2: Do not default `defaultModel` to `''`; it bypasses the gateway node's fallback model resolution and can send an empty model name. P2: The auto-created gateway node/connection is added outside the original undo transaction, so one canvas action will require multiple undos and won't round-trip cleanly through history. P2: Preserve proxy handling in the new AI Gateway branch; otherwise Anthropic gateway requests can fail in proxied deployments. P2: AI Gateway mode ignores the node's Top K and Thinking settings, so users can configure options that never affect execution. ```
Architecture diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant User participant UI as Editor UI participant Store as Pinia Store (aiGateway) participant API as Backend API (n8n) participant Engine as Workflow Execution Engine participant Node as AI Gateway Node (LangChain) participant Ext as External AI Gateway (OpenRouter) Note over User,Store: NEW: Settings Initialization User->>UI: Navigate to AI Gateway Settings UI->>Store: initialize() Store->>API: GET /rest/ai-gateway/settings API-->>Store: Return categories, default model, usage stats Store-->>UI: Populate model picker & usage charts Note over User,Store: NEW: Canvas Auto-creation Flow User->>UI: Add AI Agent Node to Canvas UI->>UI: NEW: Detect AI Language Model input UI->>Store: NEW: getGatewayLlmNodeData() alt Gateway Enabled Store-->>UI: Return lmChatN8nAiGateway node data UI->>UI: NEW: Create & Auto-connect Gateway Node UI->>UI: NEW: Inject auto-provisioned n8nAiGatewayApi credential end Note over Engine,Ext: NEW: Execution & Model Resolution Engine->>Node: supplyData() Node->>Node: getCredentials('n8nAiGatewayApi') Note right of Node: Resolution Priority:
1. Node Override
2. Workflow Settings
3. Global Default alt Manual Model Selected Node->>Node: Use specific model ID else Category Selected (Balanced, Fastest, etc.) Node->>Node: NEW: resolveAiGatewayModel(category) end Node->>Ext: POST /chat/completions (LangChain ChatOpenAI) Note over Node,Ext: Includes Base URL and API Key from Gateway Credential Ext-->>Node: Model Response Node-->>Engine: Output Data ```
Reply with feedback, questions, or to request a fix. Tag `@cubic-dev-ai` to re-run a review.

contains query parameters else Initial Request GenFn->>GenFn: Use original query params end end GenFn->>API: helpscoutApiRequest(method, endpoint, body, qs, uri) API-->>GenFn: responseData (_links.next.href) opt _links.next.href exists GenFn->>GenFn: Update uri for next iteration end end GenFn-->>Node: returnData[] Node-->>Engine: Node Output ```

body:
Good catch on the pagination query param duplication — passing `qs` alongside a pagination URI that already contains those params would double them up.

A few observations:

1. **`GenericFunctions.ts` fix is clean** — The `const requestQuery: IDataObject = uri ? {} : query;` pattern is a clear and correct way to avoid the duplication. The comment explains the intent well.

2. **`modifiedSince` array handling** — The `Array.isArray(value) ? value[0] : value` pattern in `HelpScout.node.ts` handles the date picker returning an array, which is pragmatic. However, this same logic is duplicated in two places (conversation `getAll` at line ~245 and customer `getAll` at line ~366). Consider extracting a small helper like `normalizeModifiedSince(value)` to avoid the duplication and make it easier to maintain if the date picker behavior changes.

3. **`limit` handling in `helpscoutApiRequestAllItems`** — Looking at the existing code, the `limit` check (`if (limit && returnData.length >= limit) break;`) reads from `query.limit`. Since on paginated requests you now pass `requestQuery = {}`, the limit is still read from the original `query` parameter (not `requestQuery`), so limit behavior is preserved. Good.

4. **Test coverage is thorough** — The `GenericFunctions.test.ts` covers first request params, paginated empty params, limit behavior, and multi-page accumulation. Nice.

Overall this is a solid bug fix with good test coverage.

with admin allowlist guidance else IP is allowed SSRF-->>Workflow: Validation Success Workflow->>Workflow: Proceed with network request end Note over SSRF,Error: Logic includes validation for both
initial requests and redirects (Sync/Async) ```

Added a bare catch that swallows errors when resolving node webhook ids; at minimum log the error or provide contextual diagnostics instead of silently skipping.

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​A bare catch block was added that swallows any error without performing logging, rethrowing, or any handling. Silent catch blocks hide failures and make debugging difficult, especially when the code around them performs important operations (resolving node webhook ids). The comment indicates the error is being ignored, but it provides no runtime visibility or diagnostic context. Adding minimal logging or more specific handling would improve observability while preserving the intended skip behavior.

**:wrench: How do I fix it?**
Add proper error handling in catch blocks. Log the error, show user feedback, or rethrow if needed.

body:
Added a bare catch that swallows errors when resolving node webhook ids; at minimum log the error or provide contextual diagnostics instead of silently skipping.

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​A bare catch was added that silently ignores any exception thrown while resolving webhook ids during workflow creation. Silently swallowing exceptions removes visibility into unexpected failures during a state-changing operation (creating a workflow) and makes diagnosing issues harder. Even when skipping is intended, logging at debug level or including contextual details would help with future debugging.

**:wrench: How do I fix it?**

body:
Good fix — moving `applySsoProvisioning` outside the transaction ensures the user is persisted even if provisioning fails. The test validates exactly this scenario.

One thing to consider:

**Error propagation after user creation** — After the transaction commits and the user is persisted, `applySsoProvisioning` can still throw (as the test demonstrates). Currently, `loginUser` will propagate that error to the caller. This means the user is created in the DB but the login flow fails, leaving the user in a state where they exist but may not have the correct roles/provisioning applied. On the next OIDC login attempt, `findByEmail` will find the existing user and skip creation, but `applySsoProvisioning` won't be called again (it only runs in the new-user branch).

Is there handling elsewhere for re-provisioning existing users on subsequent logins? If not, a user who hits a transient provisioning failure on first login could end up permanently under-provisioned. It might be worth either:
- Catching the provisioning error and logging a warning (so the user can at least log in)
- Or ensuring provisioning is also applied on subsequent logins for existing users

The test itself is well-structured — good use of `mockRejectedValueOnce` and verifying the DB state after the error.

    participant Auth as Task Broker Auth Service
    participant WS as Task Broker WS Server Registry

    Note over Runner, Server: Connection Lifecycle & Auth Flow

    Runner->>Server: NEW: GET /runners/_ws?id={id} (Upgrade Request)
    Note right of Runner: Header: Authorization: Bearer {token}

    alt Validation & Auth (BEFORE Upgrade)
        Server->>Server: Extract Runner ID & Token
        Server->>Auth: NEW: tryConsumeGrantToken(token)
        Auth-->>Server: Token Valid?
        
        alt Invalid / Expired Token
            Auth-->>Server: false
            Server->>Runner: NEW: Write HTTP 401/403 to Socket
            Server->>Runner: NEW: socket.destroy()
        else Valid Token
            Auth-->>Server: true
            Server->>Server: wsServer.handleUpgrade()
            Server->>WS: NEW: add(runnerId, ws)
            Server-->>Runner: 101 Switching Protocols
        end
    else Invalid Path / Missing ID
        Server->>Runner: NEW: Write HTTP 400/404 to Socket
        Server->>Runner: NEW: socket.destroy()
    end

    Note over Runner, Server: Connection Monitoring

    alt Unexpected Disconnect
        Server-XRunner: Connection Closed
        Runner->>Runner: CHANGED: onConnectionClose()
        opt isShuttingDown == false
            Runner->>Runner: NEW: process.exit(1)
        end
    else Graceful Shutdown
        Runner->>Runner: CHANGED: stop()
        Note right of Runner: Sets isShuttingDown = true
        Runner->>Server: Close WebSocket
        Runner->>Runner: CHANGED: onConnectionClose()
        Note right of Runner: No process exit triggered
    end

    Note over Server, WS: Internal Maintenance
    loop Heartbeat Check
        WS->>WS: CHANGED: startHeartbeatChecks()
        WS->>WS: check intervals (using taskRunnersConfig)
    end
```
</details>

body:
Changed /runners/_ws route behavior for plain HTTP: now returns 426 Upgrade Required instead of passing through authMiddleware/handleRequest. Restore backward-compatible responses or document the breaking change.

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​The change replaces the previous Express middleware chain for the WebSocket upgrade endpoint with a handler that returns 426 for any non-upgrade HTTP requests. External clients that previously sent plain HTTP requests to this endpoint (and relied on prior behavior such as receiving 401/403 from the auth middleware or being forwarded to taskBrokerWsServer.handleRequest) will now receive 426 Upgrade Required. This alters the observable HTTP response contract for that route.

**:wrench: How do I fix it?**
Support both old and new parameter names during transition periods. Add new optional parameters with defaults. Keep existing response fields while adding new ones. Focus on backwards compatibility.

body:
SlidingWindowRateLimiter.timestamps is a mutable, long-lived field that persists across requests; avoid implicit request-scoped state stored on server-instance objects or make the shared nature explicit.

<details>
<summary>Details</summary>

**:sparkles: AI Reasoning**
​A new SlidingWindowRateLimiter class was added with an instance property 'timestamps' that accumulates timestamps and is stored on the TaskBrokerServer instance as 'upgradeRateLimiter'. Because the rate limiter instance (and its timestamps array) lives on the server instance, it persists across all upgrade requests. This is intended as a shared limiter, but it is also request-affecting state that will be retained for the lifetime of the server. If the intention was to avoid cross-request state or to scope tracking per-connection, keeping a mutable array on a long-lived instance can cause unintended caching or cross-request interactions. The issue was introduced in this diff where the class and its field were added and then instantiated on the TaskBrokerServer, so it is within scope to flag here.

**:wrench: How do I fix it?**
Avoid storing request-specific data in module-level variables. Use request-scoped variables or explicitly mark shared caches as intentional.

<sub>Reply `@AikidoSec feedback: [FEEDBACK]` to get better review comments in the future.</sub>
<sub>Reply `@AikidoSec ignore: [REASON]` to ignore this issue.</sub>

body:
The early-return pattern is best when the condition guards the entire remainder of the function. Here it doesn't - the ```if (eventName === 'Note Hook')``` block is followed by the common response logic that runs for all events. An early return would require either:                                                                                         
  - Duplicating the response logic inside the Note Hook branch, or                                                                                                                     
  - Restructuring into something more convoluted to avoid duplication

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/ai-workflow-builder.ee/src/tools/utils/web-fetch.utils.ts:24">
P2: `normalizeUrlForComparison` does not lowercase scheme+host as its docstring claims. This causes `isUrlInUserMessages` to miss case-insensitive matches (e.g. user types `HTTPS://Example.COM`, tool checks `https://example.com`). Either update the implementation to match the documented behavior, or correct the docstring.</violation>

<violation number="2" location="packages/@n8n/ai-workflow-builder.ee/src/tools/utils/web-fetch.utils.ts:243">
P1: Custom agent: **Security Review**

**SSRF bypass via `redirect: 'follow'`**: Using `redirect: 'follow'` causes `fetch` to automatically follow redirects to potentially internal/private hosts *before* the SSRF check on the redirect target occurs. An attacker-controlled server can 302-redirect to cloud metadata endpoints (e.g., `169.254.169.254`) and the request will complete, even though the code later detects the cross-host redirect.

Use `redirect: 'manual'` and validate each redirect target with `isBlockedUrl` before following it.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/scripts/retry.mjs">

<violation number="1" location=".github/scripts/retry.mjs:33">
P1: Command parsing drops all but the last positional token, so multi-token commands can execute incorrectly.</violation>
</file>

<file name="packages/@n8n/ai-workflow-builder.ee/src/subgraphs/discovery.subgraph.ts">

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/ai-workflow-builder.ee/src/subgraphs/discovery.subgraph.ts:268">
P1: Shared mutable `plannerWebFetchState` on the class instance is not safe for concurrent graph invocations. If two requests enter `plannerNode` at overlapping times, one request's domain approvals and fetch budget can leak into the other.

Consider creating a fresh `MutableWebFetchState` inside `plannerNode` (scoped to that invocation) and passing it to a per-invocation security-manager factory, instead of reusing the instance-level object. This avoids cross-request state corruption.</violation>
</file>

P1: Custom agent: **Tests** This feature adds core functionality (computed parameter values with dependency tracking, loading states, error handling, and API integration) but includes no tests. At minimum, `loadComputedValue` should have tests covering the happy path (value is computed and `valueChanged` is called), error handling (issues are set correctly), and guard conditions (e.g., skipping when already loading). The existing `ParameterInput.test.ts` would be the natural place. P2: Handle computed-value load failures in the UI. The error is stored but never displayed, while computed fields stay disabled, so failures become silent and hard to recover from. ```

Service->>Service: getNodeType(nodeTypeAndVersion) Service->>Service: NEW: getMethod('computeMethod', methodName, nodeType) Service->>Service: getThisArg(path, additionalData, workflow) Service->>Node: NEW: Invoke computeMethod (e.g. getMergeDevCategory) opt Requires External Data (e.g. MergeDev Category lookup) Node->>Ext: Fetch account/details Ext-->>Node: details (category) end Node-->>Service: Computed Value Service-->>API: Result API-->>Store: NodeParameterValueType Store-->>UI: result UI->>UI: CHANGED: valueChanged(result) Note right of UI: NEW: Input is rendered as read-only
while computeMethod exists end alt Dependency Change Note over UI: Watcher on computeMethodDependsOn UI->>UI: NEW: Re-run loadComputedValue() end ```

<file context>
@@ -153,7 +153,7 @@ async function createContainer(
 
 	const waitStrategy = isWorker ? WORKER_WAIT_STRATEGY : MAIN_WAIT_STRATEGY;
-	const ports = hostPort ? [{ container: 5678, host: hostPort }, 5679] : [5678, 5679];
+	const ports = hostPort ? [{ container: 5678, host: hostPort }, 5679] : [5678];
 
 	container = container.withExposedPorts(...ports).withWaitStrategy(waitStrategy);
</file context>

```suggestion
	const ports = hostPort ? [{ container: 5678, host: hostPort }] : [5678];
```

author: cubic-dev-ai

body:
Looking at execution-lifecycle-hooks.ts, the hook registration per mode tells the story:

getLifecycleHooksForScalingWorker (line 569): Does NOT call hookFunctionsWorkflowEvents — workers never emit workflow-post-execute
getLifecycleHooksForScalingMain (line 596): DOES call hookFunctionsWorkflowEvents (line 607) — the main process emits workflow-post-execute after the worker reports back via JobFinishedMessage

author: cubic-dev-ai

body:
One general question - IIRC the "execution time" that gets reported today excludes time spent waiting. This is quite confusing and I think the simpler calculation here is better, but it will also be confusing to have two different metrics.

Is that something we thought about? I also have some memory that there was weird behaviours around waiting/resuming/subworkflows but that might be less relevant.
author: mfsiega

body: LGTM. I don't have so much context on our general story around metrics so I can't say how much sense this makes in the big picture, but it looks reasonable.
author: mfsiega

body: Just for my own understanding, why these labels and not others?
author: mfsiega

body: Since we want the histogram to measure wall clock duration of start -> stop. If we add in other labels like, failed/cancelled it means we would have to bucket those as well. So I was aiming for the smallest/simplest grouping.

SSRF bypass via HTTP redirect: Using `redirect: 'follow'` allows the initial fetch to complete to internal/private IPs before validation. An attacker-controlled URL can redirect to `169.254.169.254` or other internal addresses, and the request will be made before the redirect target is validated.

Use `redirect: 'manual'` and manually validate each redirect URL against `isBlockedUrl` before following it.</violation>

I think the way this is documented highlights the differences from how it works today, but that's not really necessary. We can just call this "loadPublishedWorkflowData".

I think the fallback is interesting, but we should leave it as a todo. The purpose of this indirection to load the workflow data is to allow atomically updating all triggers for a workflow at once - the fallback defeats that. So for now, this should simply retrieve the latest nodes/connections/etc from the published workflow version table.

We should document that the workflow we pass in is used for everything else.

        DB-->>PDS: Published version data (nodes/connections)
        PDS-->>LW: PublishedWorkflowData
    else Flag Disabled (Legacy Path)
        LW->>DB: CHANGED: findOne() with activeVersion relation
        DB-->>LW: WorkflowEntity (activeVersion)
    end

    LW->>Exec: runWorkflow(publishedData)

    Note over Web,Exec: Trigger / Poller Lazy-Loading Flow

    Web->>AWM: Trigger/Poller event fires
    AWM->>AWM: CHANGED: Invoke emit() callback
    
    AWM->>AWM: NEW: resolveWorkflowData() (fetch fresh version)
    
    alt NEW: Workflow Publication Service Enabled
        AWM->>PDS: getPublishedWorkflowData(workflowId)
        PDS->>DB: Fetch from workflow_published_version
        DB-->>PDS: Nodes/Connections
        PDS-->>AWM: PublishedWorkflowData
    else Flag Disabled (Legacy Path)
        AWM->>DB: Fetch from Workflow table (activeVersion)
        DB-->>AWM: WorkflowEntity
    end

    AWM->>Exec: runWorkflow(freshData)

    Note over AWM,DB: Error Handling & Cleanup
    AWM->>AWM: activateWorkflow() fails
    AWM->>PDS: NEW: Load nodes from published version for error workflow
    PDS-->>AWM: Error Workflow Data
    AWM->>Exec: executeErrorWorkflow()
```

body:
P1: The lazy import uses `mod.default`, which can be `undefined` in this CommonJS package and cause `new ivm.Isolate(...)` to crash at runtime.
Prompt for AI agents ```text Check if this issue is valid — if so, understand the root cause and fix it. At packages/nodes-base/nodes/Merge/v3/helpers/sandbox-utils.ts, line 14: The lazy import uses `mod.default`, which can be `undefined` in this CommonJS package and cause `new ivm.Isolate(...)` to crash at runtime. @@ -1,13 +1,24 @@ +async function getIvm(): Promise { + if (!_ivm) { + const mod = await import('isolated-vm'); + _ivm = mod.default; + } + return _ivm!; ```
```suggestion _ivm = (mod as { default?: IsolatedVm }).default ?? mod; ```
author: cubic-dev-ai

body: mod.default is not undefined — isolated-vm uses export = IsolatedVM (CJS-style) which maps to .default in a dynamic ESM import. The 26 combineBySql tests all pass through this code path and confirm it works.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User as Browser User
    participant VueComp as WorkflowHistory View
    participant PublishTimeline as WorkflowPublishTimelineContent Component
    participant Store as WorkflowHistory Store
    participant RESTClient as REST API Client
    participant BE as Backend Controller
    participant Service as WorkflowHistory Service
    participant DB as Database

    User->>VueComp: Navigate to Workflow History
    VueComp->>VueComp: Read query.tab parameter
    alt tab=publishTimeline
        VueComp->>PublishTimeline: Render WorkflowPublishTimelineContent
        PublishTimeline->>Store: getPublishTimeline(workflowId)
        Store->>RESTClient: GET /workflow-history/workflow/:id/publish-timeline
        RESTClient->>BE: HTTP GET request
        BE->>Service: getPublishTimeline(user, workflowId)
        Service->>DB: findWorkflowForUser (permission check)
        DB-->>Service: workflow or null
        alt workflow not found
            Service-->>BE: throw SharedWorkflowNotFoundError
            BE-->>RESTClient: 404 Not Found
            RESTClient-->>Store: API error
            Store-->>PublishTimeline: reject
            PublishTimeline->>PublishTimeline: Show error state
        else workflow found
            Service->>DB: Query WorkflowPublishHistory with joins
            Note over Service,DB: Fetch events + user + versionName
            DB-->>Service: events[] (id, workflowId, versionId, event, createdAt, user, workflowHistory.name)
            Service-->>BE: mapped events[] (incl. versionName)
            BE-->>RESTClient: JSON response
            RESTClient-->>Store: PublishTimelineEvent[]
            Store-->>PublishTimeline: resolve
        end
        PublishTimeline->>Store: getVersionFirstAdoptionDate(2.17.0)
        Store->>RESTClient: GET /instance-version-history/first-adoption
        RESTClient->>BE: HTTP GET request
        BE->>Service: getFirstAdoptionDate
        Service->>DB: Query adoption date
        DB-->>Service: date or null
        Service-->>BE: date or null
        BE-->>RESTClient: date or null
        RESTClient-->>Store: date string or null
        Store-->>PublishTimeline: resolve
        PublishTimeline->>PublishTimeline: Build TimelineEntry[] (sort, filter transient deactivations)
        alt has events
            PublishTimeline-->>User: Render timeline with published/unpublished entries
            User->>PublishTimeline: Click on published version link
            PublishTimeline-->>VueComp: emit('selectVersion', versionId)
            VueComp-->>VueComp: navigateToVersion(versionId)
        else empty
            PublishTimeline-->>User: Show empty state message
        end
    else tab=history
        VueComp->>VueComp: Render existing version history list (unchanged)
    end
```
</details>

body:
I was quite confused to see `Id` in the first two and then generic ones in the second two. It mixes resource references with workflow relationship types, while we already have `DependencyResourceType` for resources. I wonder if we could separate resource type from dependency relation, e.g.:
```ts
resourceType: 'workflow' | 'credential' | 'dataTable'
relation: 'reference' | 'workflowCall' | 'workflowParent'
```
Or at least remove `Id`, though that would be a bit less accurate than the structure above.
Also `workflowCall` vs `workflowParent` feels a bit unfortunate naming-wise - parent implies child, while call would imply something like caller/callee.

author: Joffcom

body:
**GHSA-v8w9-8mx6-g223 in hono** - medium severity
## Summary

When using `parseBody({ dot: true })` in HonoRequest, specially crafted form field names such as `__proto__.x` could create objects containing a `__proto__` property.

If the parsed result is later merged into regular JavaScript objects using unsafe merge patterns, this may lead to prototype pollution in the target object.

## Details

The `parseBody({ dot: true })` feature supports dot notation to construct nested objects from form field names.

In previous versions, the `__proto__` path segment was not filtered. As a result, specially crafted keys such as `__proto__.x` could produce objects containing `__proto__` properties.

While this behavior does not directly modify `Object.prototype` within Hono itself, it may become exploitable if the parsed result is later merged into regular JavaScript objects using unsafe merge patterns.

## Impact

Applications that merge parsed form data into regular objects using unsafe patterns (for example recursive deep merge utilities) may become vulnerable to prototype pollution.

<details><summary>Details</summary>

**Remediation** Aikido suggests bumping this package to version 4.12.7 to resolve this issue

<sub>Reply `@AikidoSec ignore: [REASON]` to ignore this issue.</sub>
[More info](https://app.aikido.dev/repositories/306852/pull_requests/26920/latest?groupId=1590)
</details>

    participant Runner as Task Runner Process
    participant BrokerSrv as Task Broker Server

    participant Auth as Auth Controller
    participant WSS as WS Server (ws)

    Note over Runner, Registry: Authentication & Connection Phase

    Runner->>BrokerSrv: POST /auth (with Secret Token)
    BrokerSrv->>Auth: createGrantToken()
    Auth-->>BrokerSrv: Grant Token (Single Use)

        alt isShuttingDown == false

        Runner->>Runner: CHANGED: isShuttingDown = true
        Runner->>WSS: Close WebSocket
        Runner->>Runner: onConnectionClose()
        Note right of Runner: No process exit triggered

    DB-->>Svc: Encrypted API Key & Status
    Svc->>Cipher: decrypt(apiKey)

    API->>Svc: updatePluginsSettings(dto)
    opt If API Key provided
        Svc->>Cipher: NEW: encrypt(dto.mergeDevApiKey)
        Cipher-->>Svc: Encrypted string
    end
    Svc->>DB: NEW: upsert keys to 'settings' table
    Svc-->>API: Updated PluginsSettingsDto
    API-->>UI: 204 No Content / 200 OK

    Note over UI, CH: Flow 2: Credential Injection (Usage)

    UI->>UI: User enables 'useManagedApiKey' in MergeDev Credential
    UI->>Svc: UI-Side Check: is Merge.dev enabled?
    alt Plugin Disabled
        UI->>UI: Show Warning Banner
    end

    Note over CH, Svc: Triggered during Node Execution or Test Connection
    CH->>CH: Decrypt standard credential data
    CH->>Svc: NEW: injectPluginManagedCredentials(type, data)
    
    alt type == 'mergeDevApi' AND data.useManagedApiKey == true
        Svc->>DB: Fetch global Merge.dev settings
        alt Plugin Enabled
            Svc->>Cipher: Decrypt global API Key
            Cipher-->>Svc: Raw Key
            Svc->>Svc: CHANGED: Overwrite data.apiKey with global key
            Svc-->>CH: Return modified credential data
        else Plugin Disabled
            Svc-->>CH: Throw UserError (Integration disabled)
        end
    else Other Credential Type
        Svc-->>CH: Return original data (No-op)
    end

    CH->>CH: Proceed with Node Execution / API Request
```

    participant CFG as Config Provider
    participant NC as N8nClient (fetch)
    participant SRV as n8n Server (REST API)
    participant FMT as Output Formatter

    Note over U,SRV: Request Lifecycle for @n8n/cli

    U->>C: Execute command (e.g., workflow list)
    
    C->>CFG: NEW: Resolve URL & API Key
    Note over CFG: Priority: Inline Flags > Env Vars > ~/.n8n-cli/config.json
    CFG-->>C: url, apiKey
    
    C->>NC: NEW: Initialize N8nClient
    
    C->>NC: Call resource method (e.g., listWorkflows)
    
    NC->>SRV: NEW: HTTPS request (Native fetch)
    Note right of NC: Header: X-N8N-API-KEY
    
    alt Success (2xx)
        SRV-->>NC: JSON Data
        NC-->>C: Result Object/Array
        
        C->>FMT: NEW: formatOutput(data, flags)
        
        opt Auto-format detection
            FMT->>FMT: Check if stdout is TTY
            Note right of FMT: Piped/Redirected? -> Default to JSON
        end
        
        opt NEW: JQ Filtering
            FMT->>FMT: applyJqFilter(data, flags.jq)
        end
        
        FMT-->>C: Formatted String (Table/JSON/ID)
        C->>U: Print to Stdout
        
    else Auth Failure (401)
        SRV-->>NC: 401 Unauthorized
        NC-->>C: Throw ApiError
        C->>U: Print Error + Hint to Stderr (Exit 2)
        
    else Connection/Other Error
        SRV-->>NC: 404 / 500 / Fetch Error
        NC-->>C: Throw ApiError
        C->>U: Print Error to Stderr (Exit 1)
    end

    Note over U,C: NEW: AI Agent Integration Flow
    U->>C: n8n-cli skill install --target=cursor
    C->>C: Read internal SKILL.md
    C->>U: Append instructions to .cursorrules (local filesystem)
```
</details>

This is the same pattern already used in `openWorkflowTemplate` (the non-JSON variant).

P1: Custom agent: **Security Review**

Missing allowlist parity with host-side `initializeGlobalContext()`: `Date`, `Math`, `RegExp`, `NaN`, `isFinite`, and `isNaN` are not wired onto `__data`. Expressions using any of these (e.g. `Math.round()`, `new Date()`, `isNaN(x)`) will silently resolve to `undefined` in the VM engine because tournament falls through to `window` (which is `{}` from the denylist).</violation>
</file>

<details>
<summary>Architecture diagram</summary>

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Host as Host Process (n8n)
    participant Reset as runtime/reset.ts
    participant Data as globalThis.__data (VM Isolate)
    participant Safe as runtime/safe-globals.ts (Proxies)
    participant Expr as Compiled Expression (Tournament)

    Note over Host,Expr: Initialization Phase (Execution Setup)

    Host->>Reset: resetDataProxies(timezone)
    Reset->>Reset: NEW: initializeBuiltins(__data)
    
    Reset->>Data: NEW: Set denylisted keys to {} (window, fetch, etc.)
    Reset->>Data: NEW: Inject SafeObject & SafeError wrappers
    Reset->>Data: NEW: Map safe native built-ins (Array, Map, JSON, etc.)
    Data-->>Reset: Context Prepared

    Note over Host,Expr: Runtime Execution Phase

    Expr->>Data: Access identifier (e.g., "Object")
    Note right of Expr: Tournament polyfill: ("Object" in this ? this : window)
    
    Data->>Safe: Intercept access via SafeObject Proxy
    
    alt Operation: Object.create(proto, props)
        Safe->>Safe: CHANGED: Strip second argument (props)
        Safe-->>Expr: Return Object.create(proto)
    else Operation: Error access
        Safe->>Safe: CHANGED: Trap defineProperty / set
        Safe->>Safe: Block stackTraceLimit, captureStackTrace
        Safe-->>Expr: Return sanitized Error
    else Standard Allowed Built-in
        Data-->>Expr: Return native reference (e.g., Array)
    end

    Note over Expr,Data: Success: ReferenceError avoided because keys exist on __data
```

body: These identifiers (`Date`, `Math`, `RegExp`, `NaN`, `isFinite`, `isNaN`) are all in tournament's `EXEMPT_IDENTIFIER_LIST` ([Constants.ts:10-18](https://github.com/n8n-io/n8n/blob/master/packages/%40n8n/tournament/src/Constants.ts#L10-L18)). Tournament never transforms them with the `VariablePolyfill` pattern, so they resolve directly from the isolate's native V8 globals. No need to wire them onto `__data`.

**8 issues found** across 677 files
