body: Now that we poll more frequently, there is a much higher likelihood that this can lead to duplicate execution continuations. The main protection we have is checking if `this.waitingExecutions[executionId] !== undefined`. Since `startExecution` removes the item from that map at the beginning before querying it from DB, there's a chance that we end up scheduling the same execution twice. We need to protect against taht
author: tomi

body: I'm not sure how accurate this actually is. `this.executionRepository.getWaitingExecutions()` can take a long time, for example couple seconds. The DB time is fetched concurrently and if it is X, when we reach this line the actual server time is X+2s. This means we would continue the execution 2s late. Is it a big deal? Probably not. However would be nice to use a more robust mechanism for this

author: shortstacked

body: Intentional narrowing. determineFinalExecutionStatus() in shared-hook-functions.ts always sets status to 'waiting' when waitTill is set — there’s no code path that writes an execution to the DB with a waitTill date and a different status. The old NOT IN ('crashed') was overly broad and could theoretically match stale rows with status error/running that still had a waitTill set, which would be incorrect to resume.
author: shortstacked

body: Fixed — added isPolling guard with try/finally. Skips the poll if the previous one hasn’t resolved yet. Only the leader polls (multi-main is handled by @OnLeaderTakeover/@OnLeaderStepdown), so this is a single-instance concern.
author: shortstacked

body: Mitigated by DbClock — server time is fetched independently via Promise.all alongside the execution query, with 60s cache + wall-clock interpolation. Also fixed localTimeAtFetch to capture after the DB round-trip so the approximation doesn’t run ahead by query latency.

body:
This doesn't now take network latency into account. If there's significant lag for the communication with the DB, let's say 3s, the cached time is 3s off. Now it's not the _that_ bad, the timers only fire later than they should. It does however cause the skew logging, which is not really correct since it's due to latency and not due to clocks having different values

We could try to mitigate this by:
account for round-trip time by stamping Date.now() both before and after the query, then offsetting serverTime by half the round-trip.

It's still not perfect, but I don't think there is a better way when your using a remote clock
author: tomi

body: Can we add error reporting for this and report it as `critical`? This is a state that should never happen
author: tomi

body:
What happens if this now fails? There is an entry in `this.waitingExecutions` so it will never be picked up again by the polling. The timer also never fires since its oneshot only. Basically the execution is now stuck forever.

One option would be to wrap this whole thing in a try..finally
author: tomi

body:
I feel both of these are unnecessary. The DBClock cache should be an internal implementation detail that outsiders shouldnt need to be concerned with.

Resetting the internal poll state also feels wrong since it should only managed by the poll implementation. If we reset it here and for whatever reason it repolls, it can cause overlapping polls. Better to have the poll method manage its state itself
author: tomi

body: Added one

<details>
<summary>Architecture diagram</summary>

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as n8n / @langchain/community
    participant Undici as Undici (HTTP/WS Client)
    participant Remote as External Web Server
    participant Internal as Internal/Metadata Service

    Note over App,Internal: Request Smuggling & SSRF Protection Flow

    App->>Undici: HTTP Request (headers, upgrade, etc.)
    Undici->>Undici: CHANGED: Validate Content-Length & CRLF
    alt Invalid Header (Smuggling attempt)
        Undici-->>App: Error (Prevent injection)
    else Valid
        Undici->>Remote: Send Sanitized Request
    end

    Remote-->>Undici: 302 Redirect to Internal IP
    Undici-->>App: Redirect target received
    
    App->>App: NEW: RecursiveUrlLoader validates redirect URL
    alt URL is Private/Internal (SSRF attempt)
        App-->>App: Block request
    else URL is Public
        App->>Undici: Follow redirect
        Undici->>Remote: Fetch new URL
    end

    Note over App,Remote: WebSocket Security Flow (DoS Mitigation)

    App->>Undici: Open WebSocket Connection
    Remote->>Undici: Send Compressed Frame (Decompression Bomb)
    
    Undici->>Undici: NEW: Verify permessage-deflate size limits
    alt Unbounded Expansion
        Undici->>Undici: Terminate connection (Prevent memory exhaustion)
    else Within Limits
        Undici-->>App: Emit 'message' event
    end

    Remote->>Undici: Send Frame with 64-bit length
    Undici->>Undici: NEW: Integer overflow check in ByteParser
    alt Invalid/Massive Length
        Undici->>Undici: Handle gracefully (Prevent process crash)
    else Valid Length
        Undici-->>App: Process payload
    end

    Note over Undici,Remote: Deduplication Interceptor

    Undici->>Undici: NEW: Response Body Streaming
    alt Large Response
        Undici-->>App: Stream chunks (Prevent OOM)
        Undici->>Undici: NEW: Disable late deduplication
    end
```
</details>

P1: Custom agent: **Security Review**

Authorization bypass risk: changing from deny-by-default to allow-by-default when `user` is present but `sharingOptions` is absent. The previous `else` branch safely denied all results; the new code silently removes all access-control filters without verifying the user actually has global scope. Any caller that omits `sharingOptions` for a non-admin user would expose all executions.

Add an explicit check for the user's global scope (e.g. `user.hasGlobalScope('workflow:list')`) before allowing unfiltered access, or keep the deny-by-default behavior and require callers to explicitly opt in to unscoped queries.</violation>
</file>

body:
**6 issues found** across 6 files

<details>
<summary>Prompt for AI agents (unresolved issues)</summary>

```text

Check if these issues are valid — if so, understand the root cause of each and fix them. If appropriate, use sub-agents to investigate and fix each issue separately.


<file name="packages/core/src/encryption/__tests__/validation.test.ts">

<violation number="1" location="packages/core/src/encryption/__tests__/validation.test.ts:156">
P2: Don't codify `RSA PRIVATE KEY` PEMs as invalid here; they're valid PEM private keys and this test would block support for them.</violation>

<violation number="2" location="packages/core/src/encryption/__tests__/validation.test.ts:236">
P2: Use a real X.509 certificate here; this test currently passes only when the "valid key pair" path throws.</violation>
</file>

<file name="packages/cli/src/modules/sso-saml/__tests__/saml.service.ee.test.ts">

<violation number="1" location="packages/cli/src/modules/sso-saml/__tests__/saml.service.ee.test.ts:1122">
P2: Use a real PEM key/certificate pair here. According to linked Linear issue IAM-388, the happy path must accept matching keys, but these "valid" fixtures are not parseable PEMs so the new tests never hit that path.</violation>

<violation number="2" location="packages/cli/src/modules/sso-saml/__tests__/saml.service.ee.test.ts:1240">
P3: This `isSignedSamlRequestsEnabled` suite duplicates the earlier block verbatim, so it adds maintenance without adding coverage.</violation>

<violation number="3" location="packages/cli/src/modules/sso-saml/__tests__/saml.service.ee.test.ts:1276">
P1: Don't re-encrypt keys that are already stored encrypted. According to linked Linear issue IAM-388, this should round-trip as encrypted-at-rest data; the expectation here locks in double-encryption on reload, which will make persisted signing keys fail validation after a restart.</violation>
</file>

<file name="packages/cli/src/modules/sso-saml/saml.service.ee.ts">

<violation number="1" location="packages/cli/src/modules/sso-saml/saml.service.ee.ts:458">
P0: Custom agent: **Security Review**

Double encryption of `signingPrivateKey` on DB reload breaks SAML signing after restart. `loadPreferencesWithoutValidation` unconditionally encrypts the key, but when called from `loadFromDbAndApplySamlPreferences` the value loaded from the database is already encrypted (it was encrypted before being saved). This double-encrypts the key, causing `getDecryptedPrivateKey()` to return encrypted gibberish instead of a PEM key, failing validation and throwing a `BadRequestError` that the `init()` error handler does not catch — potentially crashing the service on startup.

Consider adding a flag parameter (e.g., `fromDb: boolean`) or checking whether the key is already encrypted, so that encryption is only applied to plaintext keys from API input.</violation>
</file>
```

</details>

<details>
<summary>Architecture diagram</summary>

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Admin as Admin UI / API
    participant Service as SamlService (EE)
    participant Cipher as Cipher Service (Core)
    participant Valid as Validation Utils (Core)
    participant DB as Settings Repository (DB)

    Note over Admin, DB: SAML Signing Key Configuration Flow

    Admin->>Service: setSamlPreferences(prefs)
    
    Service->>Service: Check N8N_ENV_FEAT_SIGNED_SAML_REQUESTS
    
    alt Feature Flag Enabled
        opt New Keys Provided
            Service->>Cipher: NEW: encrypt(signingPrivateKey)
            Cipher-->>Service: encrypted string
            Service->>Service: Update internal state (plaintext Cert + encrypted PK)
        end

        alt authnRequestsSigned == true
            Service->>Service: NEW: getDecryptedPrivateKey()
            Service->>Cipher: decrypt(signingPrivateKey)
            Cipher-->>Service: plaintext private key
            
            Service->>Valid: NEW: validateKeyPair(privateKey, certificate)
            Valid->>Valid: Check PEM formats
            Valid->>Valid: Extract public keys & compare
            
            alt Keys Match
                Valid-->>Service: void
            else Keys Mismatch or Invalid Format
                Valid-->>Service: throw Error
                Service-->>Admin: 400 Bad Request
            end
        end

        Service->>DB: saveSamlPreferencesToDb()
        DB-->>Service: Success
        Service-->>Admin: 200 OK (Redacted PK)

    else Feature Flag Disabled
        alt signingPrivateKey or signingCertificate present
            Service-->>Admin: 400 Bad Request (Feature disabled)
        end
    end

    Note over Admin, DB: Runtime Usage (AuthnRequest)

    Service->>Service: NEW: getDecryptedPrivateKey()
    Service->>Cipher: decrypt(storedPrivateKey)
    Cipher-->>Service: plaintext key
    Service->>Service: CHANGED: Initialize Samlify with signing key
    Service->>Admin: Redirect to IdP (Signed Request)
```
</details>

author: afitzek

body: Could a race condition happen here? If we start multiple executeSearch runs we await the result and once it is back, we write the result of this query. If a different newer query was quicker, we would overwrite the results from the older query. Maybe `useDebounceFn` takes care of this, but even if it does, the onMounted call, would be in depended from this? Should we "store" the latest query id, and only write results of the current scope is still the latest id?
author: afitzek

body:
agree, with the race condition and the fix. i'm implementing it now.

the right way of doing this would be with `AbortSignal` but i'll have to plum it through 4 layers... going with the quick fix

author: afitzek

body:
Can you help me making this more intuitive with a different name? 

In my mind it makes sense as it is, but maybe I'm too deep in it. 😅 This is an argument for the the calling function, so the calling function can tell the redaction system, if the redaction system should create a copy if it needs to redact something. Basically this is a memory optimization, because the redaction system determines if a redaction is necessary, if it is not necessary we can safe the deep clone in this new use case that this PR is adding.

There are basically two cases (which are independent of the redaction policy):

1. We want to mutate this in place, this happens when we fetch an execution from the db to send it to the user. To safe memory, we don't want to copyOnWrite, it should mutate the data in place.

2. We are sending the execution to a user, but the execution data is still being used (because it is an active execution), so we don't want to mutate the execution data, because this is still in use. In this case we only want to create a copy in memory, if there are changes to the data.

P1: Attachment downloads can still abort the node even when continueOnFail is enabled. According to linked Linear issue NODE-3645, the download phase also needs per-item error handling so one broken attachment returns `{ error }` instead of stopping the remaining items.</violation>
</file>
```

</details>

<details>
<summary>Architecture diagram</summary>

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Engine as n8n Workflow Engine
    participant Node as Jira Node
    participant API as Jira REST API
    participant Helper as Node Helpers

    Engine->>Node: execute()
    
    loop For each Input Item
        Node->>Node: Get parameters (Issue Key, Summary, etc.)
        
        Note over Node,API: Request execution
        Node->>API: jiraSoftwareCloudApiRequest()
        
        alt API Success
            API-->>Node: JSON Response
            Node->>Helper: constructExecutionMetaData(Response)
            Node->>Node: Push to returnData
        else NEW: API Error / Validation Error
            API-->>Node: Error (4xx/5xx or Network)
            
            alt NEW: continueOnFail is TRUE
                Node->>Node: Catch Error
                Node->>Helper: NEW: constructExecutionMetaData({ error: message })
                Node->>Node: Push error item to returnData
                Note right of Node: Loop continues to next item
            else continueOnFail is FALSE (Default)
                Node-->>Engine: Throw NodeOperationError
                Note right of Engine: Workflow execution stops
            end
        end
    end

    Node-->>Engine: return returnData (Array of Success + Error items)
```
</details>

<sub>Reply with feedback, questions, or to request a fix. Tag `@cubic-dev-ai` to re-run a review.</sub>

		const results = await Promise.all(
			Array.from(byBenchmark, async ([benchmarkName, metrics]) => {
				const payload = { ...context, benchmark_name: benchmarkName, metrics };
				try {
					const response = await fetch(this.webhookUrl, {
						method: 'POST',
						headers: {
							'Content-Type': 'application/json',
							Authorization: `Basic ${auth}`,
						},
						body: JSON.stringify(payload),
						signal: AbortSignal.timeout(30000),
					});

					if (!response.ok) {
						console.warn(
							`[MetricsReporter] Webhook failed (${response.status}) for "${benchmarkName}": ${metrics.length} metrics dropped`,
						);
						return 0;
					}

					return metrics.length;
				} catch (e) {
					console.warn(
						`[MetricsReporter] Failed to send metrics for "${benchmarkName}": ${(e as Error).message}`,
					);
					return 0;
				}
			}),
		);
		sent = results.reduce((total, count) => total + count, 0);

If appropriate, use sub-agents to investigate and fix each issue separately. P1: This `appSecret` field is not wired into the OAuth2 path. OAuth2 trigger requests never compute `appsecret_proof`, and webhook signature validation still loads `facebookGraphAppApi`, so the secret configured here has no effect. P2: Add a `test` request for the new OAuth2 credential so misconfigured Facebook OAuth2 connections are caught during credential validation. (Based on your team's feedback about adding tests for new or changed credentials.) [FEEDBACK_USED] P2: This reimplements Facebook auth handling in the node instead of reusing the shared helper/credential flow, so the access-token and OAuth2 paths can drift the next time auth behavior changes. (Based on your team's feedback about using HTTP helpers with built-in authentication.) [FEEDBACK_USED] P2: Add a credential `test` request for this new OAuth2 credential; right now the suite only checks metadata, but the credential itself cannot be validated from the UI. (Based on your team's feedback about adding tests for new or changed credentials.) [FEEDBACK_USED] P2: Add a `test` request for this new OAuth2 credential so the editor can validate the Facebook connection before runtime. (Based on your team's feedback about adding tests for new or changed credentials.) [FEEDBACK_USED] P2: These flow tests bypass the credential’s real OAuth config, so they can pass even if the credential starts requesting the wrong scopes. P1: OAuth2 trigger requests now use `facebookGraphAppOAuth2Api`, but the webhook handler still requires `facebookGraphAppApi`. An OAuth2-configured Facebook Trigger can therefore fail when the webhook fires. ```
Architecture diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant User as User/Browser participant UI as n8n UI participant Workflow as n8n Workflow Engine participant Helpers as n8n Core Helpers participant FB as Facebook Graph API Note over User, FB: Credential Setup (OAuth2 Flow) User->>UI: Select OAuth2 Authentication UI->>FB: Redirect to /dialog/oauth FB-->>User: Request Permissions (Scopes) User->>FB: Approve FB-->>UI: Return Auth Code UI->>FB: POST /oauth/access_token (code + secret) FB-->>UI: Return Access Token + Refresh Token UI->>UI: Store Credentials (NEW: facebookGraphApiOAuth2Api) Note over Workflow, FB: Runtime Execution (Node/Trigger) Workflow->>Workflow: Start Execution Workflow->>Workflow: NEW: Determine authType (OAuth2 vs Access Token) alt authType == 'oAuth2' Workflow->>Helpers: NEW: requestWithAuthentication(credentialName, options) Note right of Helpers: credentialName: facebookGraphApiOAuth2Api
OR facebookGraphAppOAuth2Api (Trigger) Helpers->>Helpers: Retrieve & Refresh Token Helpers->>FB: Request with Authorization: Bearer {token} Note right of FB: CHANGED: No 'access_token' in Query Params
CHANGED: No 'appsecret_proof' calculation FB-->>Helpers: JSON Response Helpers-->>Workflow: Data else authType == 'accessToken' Workflow->>Workflow: Get Manual Access Token from Creds Workflow->>FB: Request with ?access_token={token} opt appSecret provided Workflow->>Workflow: Generate HMAC appsecret_proof Workflow->>FB: Include ?appsecret_proof={hash} end FB-->>Workflow: JSON Response end Workflow-->>User: Display Results (UI) Note over Workflow, FB: UNHAPPY PATH (Token Expired) alt Token Invalid/Expired Helpers->>FB: Request with Bearer Token FB-->>Helpers: 401 Unauthorized Helpers->>FB: Refresh Token Flow FB-->>Helpers: New Access Token Helpers->>FB: Retry Original Request FB-->>Workflow: Data end ```

body:
Is there a reason to use the `v19.0` scoped url here and for access token url? In the docs I can see that the latest version is `v25`: https://developers.facebook.com/docs/graph-api/changelog/version25.0

The official wellknown endpoint specify the authorization url without version: https://www.facebook.com/.well-known/openid-configuration/
So do the docs with the access token url: https://developers.facebook.com/docs/facebook-login/guides/access-tokens/?locale=en_US#generating-an-app-access-token (normally the access token url is also in well known, Facebook does not seem to take the spec that seriously)

**3 issues found** across 6 files

(Based on your team's feedback about adding test requests for new or changed credentials.) [FEEDBACK_USED] P2: Require `customDomainUrl` when custom domains are enabled. Otherwise the toggle can be turned on with an empty value and requests silently fall back to the non-site-scoped base URL. P1: The OAuth2 test locks in the wrong base URL for non-custom-domain WordPress.com sites. Those sites should use their own `/wp-json/wp/v2/...` endpoint, not `public-api.wordpress.com/wp/v2/...` without a site identifier. ```
Architecture diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant WF as Workflow Executor participant Node as Wordpress Node participant Core as n8n Core (Auth) participant WPCOM_Auth as WordPress.com OAuth participant WPCOM_API as WordPress.com Public API participant Self_API as Self-Hosted WordPress API Note over WF,Self_API: Request Flow with New OAuth2 Support WF->>Node: execute() Node->>Node: CHANGED: getParameter("authType") alt authType == "oAuth2" (NEW) Node->>Core: getCredentials("wordpressOAuth2Api") opt No Token / Expired Core->>WPCOM_Auth: Exchange Code/Refresh for Access Token WPCOM_Auth-->>Core: Access Token end Core-->>Node: Credential Data (Token, customDomain settings) Node->>Node: NEW: Construct Base URI Note right of Node: Default: https://public-api.wordpress.com/wp/v2
If custom domain: /sites/{customDomainUrl} Node->>WPCOM_API: NEW: requestWithAuthentication(credentialType, options) Note right of WPCOM_API: Header: Authorization: Bearer {token} WPCOM_API-->>Node: API Response else authType == "basicAuth" (Legacy Path) Node->>Core: getCredentials("wordpressApi") Core-->>Node: Credential Data (URL, Credentials, allowUnauthorizedCerts) Node->>Node: Construct URI: {url}/wp-json/wp/v2 Node->>Self_API: requestWithAuthentication(credentialType, options) Note right of Self_API: Header: Authorization: Basic {base64} Self_API-->>Node: API Response end Node-->>WF: Output Data (Posts, Pages, etc.) ```
Reply with feedback, questions, or to request a fix. Tag `@cubic-dev-ai` to re-run a review.

body: Thanks for the feedback! I've [updated an existing learning](https://www.cubic.dev/ai-review?repo=193215554&tab=learnings&feedbackId=29ec68c4-cc71-4b06-ab62-b36feeff3850) with this new information.
author: cubic-dev-ai

body: For user-authorization OAuth2 flows like WordPress.com's, the auth flow itself is the test: the credential is only usable after the user completes the OAuth2 redirect dance and receives an access token. Adding a test request here would be adding something that 65 out of 66 similar credentials don't have, and it would require the token to already exist before it can be tested — which is circular.
author: Joffcom

body: Thanks for the feedback! I've [updated an existing learning](https://www.cubic.dev/ai-review?repo=193215554&tab=learnings&feedbackId=29ec68c4-cc71-4b06-ab62-b36feeff3850) with this new information.
author: cubic-dev-ai

body:
When `customDomain` is false, `sitePrefix` is empty and the URL becomes `.../wp/v2/posts` — but the WordPress.com API always requires `/sites/{site}/` in the path. This would fail for regular `.wordpress.com` subdomain users too.

Would it make sense to always require a site identifier for OAuth2, rather than gating it behind the custom domain toggle? Something like a single "WordPress.com Site" field that accepts either `myblog.wordpress.com` or `myblog.com`.
author: DawidMyslak

body: The basic auth credential has a `test` request so users can validate their connection from the UI. Would be great to add one here too — e.g. a GET to `/users/me` on the WordPress.com API.
author: DawidMyslak

body: Nit: the catch branch only strips trailing slashes, so `myblog.com/path` (no protocol) would keep the `/path`. A `.split('/')[0]` would handle that edge case.
author: DawidMyslak

body: This test locks in a URL without the `/sites/{site}` segment — would need updating once the base URL construction is fixed.
author: DawidMyslak

body: The OAuth2 flow tests here are mostly exercising `@n8n/client-oauth2` rather than the credential itself. The property assertions are great — maybe trim the flow tests in favor of testing the scope expression logic?

**5 issues found** across 11 files
Prompt for AI agents (unresolved issues) ```text Check if these issues are valid — if so, understand the root cause of each and fix them. If appropriate, use sub-agents to investigate and fix each issue separately. P0: Custom agent: **Security Review** Security Review / Credential & Secret Handling: this new reusable workflow executes repo-controlled `pnpm install` and `node` steps after minting the GitHub App token and inheriting secrets. A merged release PR can therefore run arbitrary code with the write-scoped app token and inherited secrets. P2: Drop the unused `id-token: write` permission. This job only uses the generated GitHub App token, so keeping OIDC enabled grants broader credentials than necessary. P1: Only treat a 404 as "release missing" here; swallowing every GitHub API error can skip the delete step and then fail the pointer-release update for `stable`/`beta`/`v1`. P2: `bundler` resolution no longer matches how these `.mjs` scripts are executed. Since GitHub Actions runs them with plain `node`, keep a Node-style resolution mode so `checkJs` continues catching Node ESM import issues before release jobs run. P1: According to linked Linear issue CAT-2564, this flow needs to recreate the `stable`/`beta`/`v1` pointer releases, not just mark a versioned release as latest. Updating `n8n@x.y.z` here leaves the track releases unchanged or missing. ```
Architecture diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant WF as Release Workflow (YAML) participant Auth as GitHub App (n8n-assistant) participant JS as Node.js Script (.mjs) participant GH as GitHub REST API Note over WF, GH: Release Creation Flow (triggered by Merge/Dispatch) WF->>Auth: NEW: Request Installation Token Auth-->>WF: GITHUB_TOKEN WF->>JS: Execute create-github-release.mjs Note right of JS: Env: RELEASE_TAG, ADDITIONAL_TAGS,
BODY, COMMIT, IS_PRE_RELEASE loop For each tag (RELEASE_TAG + ADDITIONAL_TAGS) JS->>GH: CHANGED: getExistingRelease(tag) GH-->>JS: release data / 404 alt NEW: Is Release Track Tag (e.g., 'v1') AND exists JS->>GH: NEW: deleteRelease(id) GH-->>JS: 204 No Content end JS->>GH: CHANGED: createRelease(tag, commit, body, latest) GH-->>JS: 201 Created (Release Object) end JS-->>WF: NEW: writeGithubOutput(release_urls) Note over WF, GH: Release Promotion Flow (triggered by Minor Bumps) opt release_type != 'rc' AND bump == 'minor' WF->>JS: NEW: Execute promote-github-release.mjs JS->>GH: NEW: getExistingRelease(RELEASE_TAG) GH-->>JS: release data JS->>GH: NEW: updateRelease(id, make_latest: true) GH-->>JS: 200 OK JS-->>WF: writeGithubOutput(release_url) end ```

body: Can you give an example of an expression where there would cause an incorrect dependency mapping
author: ireneea

body: @ireneea aee6b9fde641f8dc5092b29bad4d29dddc6dc722 I think this covers it :) with the test
author: alielkhateeb

body:
@ireneea An example of an expression that would cause an incorrect dependency mapping is:

`{{ $secrets.vault-production.apiKey }}`

If the regex used to extract the provider key was too simple—for example, if it only matched alphanumeric characters (`[A-Za-z0-9]+`) and didn't account for hyphens—it would stop at the first dash and extract **`vault`** instead of **`vault-production`**. 

If your environment has a secret provider named "vault" and another named "vault-production", the system would incorrectly link the credential to the "vault" provider. This would mean:
* Deleting "vault" might trigger warnings or logic related to this credential, even though it doesn't use it.
* Deleting "vault-production" might seem "safe" to the system because no dependencies were correctly recorded.

I suggested the fix in `external-secrets.utils.ts` (adding the `(?=\\.)` lookahead) to ensure we only capture the provider key when it is followed by the dot used for property access. This prevents capturing partial strings or false positives from text that happens to start with `$secrets.` but isn't a valid secret reference.

Note that the migration in `packages/@n8n/db/src/migrations/common/1773000000000-CreateCredentialDependencyTable.ts` still lacks this lookahead, so it might be worth applying the same logic there to ensure the backfill is as accurate as the runtime logic.

[Credentials Service Tests](packages/cli/src/credentials/__tests__/credentials.service.test.ts) | [External Secrets Utils](packages/cli/src/credentials/external-secrets.utils.ts)
author: cubic-dev-ai

body:
@ireneea I suggested this because the current regex is restricted to alphanumeric characters and hyphens, but it doesn't use word boundaries or lookaheads to ensure it has captured the *entire* intended provider key.

An incorrect mapping occurs if a provider key contains a character that the regex doesn't recognize (like an underscore), but the first part of that key *does* match the regex.

### Example: Partial Extraction with Underscores
Suppose you have an external secret provider named `vault_production`.

If you use this expression:
`={{ $secrets.vault_production.apiKey }}`

1. The regex `\$secrets\.([A-Za-z0-9]+(?:-[A-Za-z0-9]+)*)` is applied.
2. It matches `$secrets.`.
3. It then matches `vault` as the first group of `[A-Za-z0-9]+`.
4. It encounters the underscore `_`. Since underscores are not allowed in the hyphenated group `(?:-[A-Za-z0-9]+)*`, the regex engine stops matching.
5. **Result:** The service extracts `vault` as the dependency ID.

If you happen to have another provider simply named `vault`, n8n will incorrectly link this credential to the `vault` provider instead of `vault_production`. If no `vault` provider exists, the dependency won't be tracked at all, even though the expression is active.

### Why this happens
The `matchAll` logic finds the longest prefix that satisfies the pattern. Without a boundary check (like `\b`) or a requirement that the next character must be a dot or bracket, it greedily takes whatever partial string fits.

This also affects bracket notation differently. If you wrote `{{ $secrets['vault_production']['key'] }}`, the regex would fail to match entirely because the closing `['` wouldn't be found immediately after the partial match `vault`. This inconsistency makes debugging dependency issues difficult.

You can find the relevant regex definition at packages/cli/src/credentials/external-secrets.utils.ts.
author: cubic-dev-ai

body: Does this https://github.com/n8n-io/n8n/pull/27151#discussion_r2959607542 cover the case you mentioned? Any regressions in doing that?
author: alielkhateeb

body:
@alielkhateeb The fix in `external-secrets.utils.ts` (adding the `(?=\\.)` lookahead) successfully covers the partial extraction case for dot notation.

### Does it cover the case?
Yes. By adding the positive lookahead `(?=\\.)`, you ensure that the regex only captures the provider key if it is immediately followed by a dot. 

*   **Example:** `{{ $secrets.vault_production.apiKey }}`
    *   The `PROVIDER_KEY_PATTERN` matches `vault`.
    *   The regex engine then checks the lookahead. Since the character following `vault` is `_` (and not `.`), the match fails.
    *   This prevents the incorrect mapping to a provider named `vault`.

### Potential Regressions
There is one edge case that could be considered a regression: **referencing the provider object without immediate property access.**

If a user writes an expression where they don't immediately access a key with a dot, such as:
*   `{{ Object.keys($secrets.myVault) }}`
*   `{{ $secrets.myVault ? 'connected' : 'missing' }}`

In these instances, `myVault` is a valid dependency, but the lookahead `(?=\\.)` will cause the regex to skip it because there is no trailing dot. While `$secrets.provider.key` is the standard usage pattern in n8n, this change technically makes the detection slightly more restrictive. 

A safer alternative that avoids this regression while still fixing the partial match issue would be using a word boundary or a negative lookahead to ensure the name has ended:
`new RegExp(`\\$secrets\\.(${PROVIDER_KEY_PATTERN})\\b`, 'g')`

### Important: Migration Sync
The migration in `packages/@n8n/db/src/migrations/common/1773000000000-CreateCredentialDependencyTable.ts` (lines 144-146) still uses the old regex:

```typescript
const dotMatches = expressionContent.matchAll(
    new RegExp(`\\$secrets\\.(${providerKeyPattern})`, 'g'),
);
```

You should apply your chosen fix here as well. If you don't, the backfill during the migration will still create incorrect dependencies for any existing credentials using underscores or other non-pattern characters.

[External Secrets Utils](packages/cli/src/credentials/external-secrets.utils.ts) | [Credential Dependency Migration](packages/@n8n/db/src/migrations/common/1773000000000-CreateCredentialDependencyTable.ts)

body: Why the separate createIndex ? why not another `.withIndexOn(` ?
author: guillaumejacquart

body:
That’s an absolutely valid question ✅

The main reason is that I found it hard to clearly spot unique indexes when they’re defined alongside non-unique ones using `withIndexOn`. For example:

```ts
.withIndexOn(['credentialId'])
.withIndexOn(['dependencyType', 'dependencyId'])
.withIndexOn(['credentialId', 'dependencyType', 'dependencyId'], true);
```

Here, the `true` is quite subtle and easy to miss when scanning the code.

Since this is a migration file (and not something we expect to revisit or refactor), I preferred to make the unique index explicit. This makes it easier for reviewers and future readers to spot which index enforces uniqueness vs which ones are just for performance.

Also… the fact that you pointed it out probably means my plan worked 😂 haha

sequenceDiagram
    participant GH as GitHub Workflow

        alt NEW: version < CURRENT_MAJOR_VERSION (e.g., 1.x)
            H-->>BM: Return simple branch name ("1.x")
        else version >= CURRENT_MAJOR_VERSION (e.g., 2.x)
            H-->>BM: Return prefixed name ("release-candidate/2.x.x")
        end

        BM->>H: remoteBranchExists(branchName)
        H->>Git: git ls-remote --heads origin
        Git-->>H: branch existence status
        H-->>BM: boolean

            H->>Git: git rev-parse
            Git-->>H: commit hash
            BM->>H: sh('git push origin ...')
        end
    end

```

body:
**40 issues found** across 103 files

**Note:** This PR contains a large number of files. cubic only reviews up to 75 files per PR, so some files may not have been reviewed.

<details>
<summary>Prompt for AI agents (unresolved issues)</summary>

```text

Check if these issues are valid — if so, understand the root cause of each and fix them. If appropriate, use sub-agents to investigate and fix each issue separately.


<file name=".github/workflows/backport.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/backport.yml:39">
P0: Custom agent: **Security Review**

This workflow executes checked-out `.github` code after minting a write-capable GitHub App token. On `pull_request.closed`, that lets merged PR content in `.github/actions` or `.github/scripts` run with privileged credentials.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/docker-build-smoke.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/docker-build-smoke.yml:16">
P2: The PR path filter misses this workflow and its local composite actions, so changes to the smoke-test logic can bypass the check entirely.</violation>

<violation number="2" location=".github/workflows/docker-build-smoke.yml:42">
P1: `pnpm build:docker:clean` is not defined on this branch, so the smoke-test build step will fail before it exercises the Docker chain.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/security-trivy-scan-callable.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/security-trivy-scan-callable.yml:56">
P1: These new `security/` paths do not exist on 1.x, so the workflow will miss its Trivy config/VEX files and fail when Trivy tries to load `security/trivy.yaml`.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/release-create-minor-pr.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/release-create-minor-pr.yml:14">
P1: This backported release workflow still cuts the release from `master` instead of `1.x`, so it will generate release PRs from the wrong branch.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/scripts/determine-version-info.mjs">

<violation number="1" location=".github/scripts/determine-version-info.mjs:37">
P1: This fallback hard-codes unmatched releases to `beta`, so the first release of a new `v1` minor is rejected against the 2.x beta tag instead of being published on the `v1` track.</violation>

<violation number="2" location=".github/scripts/determine-version-info.mjs:54">
P1: Use the `v1` special-case when computing `rc_branch`; otherwise v1 patch releases merge back to a non-existent `release-candidate/1.*.x` branch.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/ci-master.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/ci-master.yml:46">
P1: This adds a `performance` job that currently points to a broken reusable workflow, so `ci-master` will start failing on every push.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/sec-publish-fix.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/sec-publish-fix.yml:43">
P1: Creating a PR here without syncing the public cherry-pick back to `n8n-private` leaves the two `master` branches divergent after a successful publish.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/sec-poutine-reusable.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/sec-poutine-reusable.yml:42">
P1: Keep `always()` in this condition, otherwise SARIF upload is skipped whenever the previous findings check exits with `1`. That drops the code-scanning artifact on the failure path where it is most needed.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/util-update-node-popularity.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/util-update-node-popularity.yml:87">
P1: This backport will auto-merge the generated popularity update PR into `master`, because the workflow still creates it with `base: master` on the `1.x` branch.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/scripts/send-metrics.mjs">

<violation number="1" location=".github/scripts/send-metrics.mjs:76">
P1: Don't let telemetry webhook failures fail the CI job; handle fetch errors as a non-fatal warning.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/ci-pull-requests.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/ci-pull-requests.yml:206">
P1: Don't treat skipped jobs as success in the required-checks validator. A broken `if:` condition or filter output can skip a required job and still let `Required Checks` pass.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/release-merge-tag-to-branch.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/release-merge-tag-to-branch.yml:53">
P1: Use `--ff-only` here. `--ff` still permits a non-fast-forward merge commit, which can silently rewrite the branch history this workflow is meant to advance to the release tag.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/docker-build-push.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/docker-build-push.yml:366">
P1: The VEX file path `security/vex.openvex.json` doesn't exist on this branch. The file is at the repository root as `vex.openvex.json`. This will cause the VEX attestation steps to fail.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/util-claude-task.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/util-claude-task.yml:60">
P1: Checking out the user-supplied ref here makes the workflow depend on whatever `.github` files exist in that target ref. Running this against an older tag/SHA can break `setup-nodejs` or the helper scripts before Claude starts. Keep workflow tooling pinned to the workflow branch and checkout the target ref separately.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/sbom-generation-callable.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/sbom-generation-callable.yml:71">
P1: This backport points at a VEX path that does not exist on 1.x release tags, so the release step will fail to upload the VEX file.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/release-standalone-package.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/release-standalone-package.yml:12">
P2: This release option points to a package that does not exist on this branch, so choosing it will make the build/publish filters fail. Remove it until the workspace is backported.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/scripts/send-docker-stats.mjs">

<violation number="1" location=".github/scripts/send-docker-stats.mjs:22">
P2: This script reads `docker-build-manifest.json`, but the 1.x Docker build flow does not generate that file, so the new Docker telemetry is effectively dead.</violation>

<violation number="2" location=".github/scripts/send-docker-stats.mjs:41">
P2: This parser does not match the 1.x `build-manifest.json` schema, so the compiled artifact metrics will be wrong or invalid.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/release-push-to-channel.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/release-push-to-channel.yml:59">
P2: Validate `release-channel` explicitly for reusable calls. Right now any string is accepted, which lets the workflow succeed without publishing any tags when the caller passes a typo or unsupported channel.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/scripts/claude-task/resume-callback.mjs">

<violation number="1" location=".github/scripts/claude-task/resume-callback.mjs:45">
P2: Add a timeout to the resume callback request. Without one, a slow or hung resume endpoint can stall this workflow until the job-level timeout.

(Based on your team's feedback about long-lived async operations and timeouts.) [FEEDBACK_USED]</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/scripts/detect-new-packages.mjs">

<violation number="1" location=".github/scripts/detect-new-packages.mjs:37">
P2: Don't fail open when the npm lookup can't verify a package. A transient registry error currently only logs a warning, so this check can exit 0 and miss a new package until the later publish step fails.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/scripts/send-version-release-notification.mjs">

<violation number="1" location=".github/scripts/send-version-release-notification.mjs:11">
P2: Add a timeout to this webhook request so a stalled endpoint cannot hang the release workflow.</violation>

<violation number="2" location=".github/scripts/send-version-release-notification.mjs:23">
P2: Checking for `status !== 200` will fail valid webhook responses like `201` or `204`; use `response.ok` so all 2xx statuses are treated as success.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/scripts/compute-backport-targets.mjs">

<violation number="1" location=".github/scripts/compute-backport-targets.mjs:73">
P2: `parseInt()` accepts malformed PR IDs with trailing characters, so a manual backport run can target the wrong pull request instead of failing.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/actions/setup-nodejs/action.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/actions/setup-nodejs/action.yml:55">
P2: `sha256sum` is not available on macOS by default. Since the step runs when `runner.os != 'Windows'` (includes macOS), use a cross-platform approach with `shasum -a 256 -c -` or add OS detection.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/actions/ci-filter/ci-filter.mjs">

<violation number="1" location=".github/actions/ci-filter/ci-filter.mjs:77">
P2: Strip the optional YAML list marker from multiline patterns. As written, workflows that pass `- pattern` entries never match and their filtered jobs stay disabled.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/ci-restrict-private-merges.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/ci-restrict-private-merges.yml:6">
P2: This backported workflow only listens for PRs into `master`, so it never enforces the `bundle/*` restriction on `1.x` PRs.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/ci-check-release-from-fork.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/ci-check-release-from-fork.yml:4">
P2: `pull_request` runs from forks only get a read-only token, so this comment step cannot post the explanatory PR message for the blocked fork PRs.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/util-cleanup-abandoned-release-branches.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/util-cleanup-abandoned-release-branches.yml:11">
P2: Narrow this job guard before using app secrets. With the current `merged == false` check, any unmerged fork PR will still try to run `create-github-app-token`, but `pull_request` workflows from forks do not receive repository secrets, so the workflow fails before the cleanup script can no-op.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/build-windows.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/build-windows.yml:47">
P2: This smoke test never exercises the built CLI: `--version` exits before any compiled code is loaded, so Windows startup/build regressions will still pass CI.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/workflows/util-data-tooling.yml">

<violation number="1" location=".github/workflows/util-data-tooling.yml:27">
P2: Comparing this backported workflow against `master` makes the DB filter incorrect on `1.x`; manual runs will pick up branch divergence instead of changes relative to the release branch.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/scripts/github-helpers.mjs">

<violation number="1" location=".github/scripts/github-helpers.mjs:253">
P2: Remove the shell-style quote from `--format`; with `execFileSync` it is emitted as a literal `"` in every commit line.</violation>
</file>

<file name=".github/WORKFLOWS.md">

<violation number="1" location=".github/WORKFLOWS.md:176">
P2: This entry references `test-db.yml`, but that workflow file does not exist on this branch. The docs should keep pointing to the actual database workflow name, otherwise maintainers following the runbook will look for a missing file.</violation>

<violation number="2" location=".github/WORKFLOWS.md:597">
P2: These new `security/...` paths do not exist on this branch. The doc should keep referencing the actual VEX and Trivy files (`vex.openvex.json` and `.github/trivy.yaml`) so contributors do not follow broken paths.</violation>

<violation number="3" location=".github/WORKFLOWS.md:662">
P3: This sentence is no longer accurate on this branch: `/build-unit-test` is still implemented via `build-unit-test-pr-comment.yml`, so the docs should use plural wording or list both comment-trigger workarounds.</violation>

<file name=".github/scripts/get-release-versions.mjs">

<violation number="1" location=".github/scripts/get-release-versions.mjs:17">

<file name=".github/CI-TELEMETRY.md">

<violation number="1" location=".github/CI-TELEMETRY.md:37">

<file name=".github/scripts/update-changelog.mjs">

```mermaid

sequenceDiagram
    participant GHA as GitHub Actions Runner
    participant Filter as NEW: CI Filter (Node.js)
    participant Setup as CHANGED: Setup Action
    participant SC as NEW: Aikido SafeChain
    participant Script as CHANGED: Build/Docker Scripts
    participant Webhook as n8n Telemetry Webhook
    participant BQ as BigQuery (Storage)
    participant Registry as External Registries (GHCR/DH)

    Note over GHA, Filter: PR Triggered or Merge Group
    GHA->>Filter: NEW: runFilter(mode=filter)
    Filter->>Filter: Match changed files vs patterns
    Filter-->>GHA: Return job results (JSON)

    opt Job is "ci" or "unit-test"
        GHA->>Setup: setup-nodejs()
        Setup->>SC: NEW: install-safe-chain.sh
        SC->>SC: Enable Supply Chain Protection
        Setup->>GHA: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
        Setup->>SC: Disable SafeChain (Teardown)
        
        GHA->>Script: pnpm build --summarize
        Script->>Script: CHANGED: Parse .turbo/runs summary
        Script->>Webhook: NEW: sendMetrics (Unified Payload)
        Note right of Script: Includes Git, CI, & Runner context
        Webhook->>BQ: CHANGED: Insert into qa_performance_metrics
    end

    opt Docker Build Flow
        GHA->>Registry: CHANGED: Login with retry.mjs
        GHA->>Script: build-n8n.mjs / dockerize-n8n.mjs
        Script->>Script: Generate manifest
        Script->>Webhook: NEW: sendMetrics (Docker stats)
        GHA->>GHA: NEW: Generate SLSA L3 Provenance
        GHA->>Registry: Push multi-arch image + attestations
    end

    GHA->>Filter: NEW: runValidate(mode=validate)
    alt Any required jobs failed/cancelled
        Filter-->>GHA: Exit 1 (Fail Workflow)
    else All required checks passed
        Filter-->>GHA: Success
    end

    Note over GHA, Registry: Post-Release / Cleanup
    opt On PR Close (Abandoned)
        GHA->>Script: NEW: cleanup-release-branch.mjs
    end
    opt Scheduled (Daily)
        GHA->>Script: NEW: cleanup-ghcr-images.mjs --stale 1
    end
```

body:
**CVE-2026-33750 in brace-expansion** - low severity
The brace-expansion library generates arbitrary strings containing a common prefix and suffix. Prior to versions 5.0.5, 3.0.2, 2.0.3, and 1.1.13, a brace pattern with a zero step value (e.g., `{1..2..0}`) causes the sequence generation loop to run indefinitely, making the process hang for seconds and allocate heaps of memory. Versions 5.0.5, 3.0.2, 2.0.3, and 1.1.13 fix the issue. As a workaround, sanitize strings passed to `expand()` to ensure a step value of `0` is not used.

<details><summary>Details</summary>

**Remediation** Aikido suggests bumping this package to version 5.0.5 to resolve this issue

<sub>Reply `@AikidoSec ignore: [REASON]` to ignore this issue.</sub>
[More info](https://app.aikido.dev/repositories/306852/pull_requests/27154/latest?groupId=1590)
</details>

body:
Node code is trusted server-side code. In n8n, nodes are either built-in or installed by the instance
  owner. A node that passes a different workflowId is no different from any other server-side code — it
  could already make direct DB queries, read files, or call any injected service. There's no sandbox
  boundary between nodes.

body:
Yes - I think we're adding a `started` log before the credentials have been checked so it could `throw` on line 162.

Maybe do it at the end of the function?
Also, should we make it a `.debug` rather than `.info` to avoid spamming the logs?
author: MarcL

body:
Ah - is this just duplicating the same message inside `job-processor.ts`?
It logs the start of the execution (line 95).
author: MarcL

body:
@MarcL In queue mode, these are distinct events logged by different processes. `WorkflowRunner` logs on the **Main** process when the execution is first registered and enqueued, while `JobProcessor` logs on the **Worker** when processing actually begins. 

However, in production environments running queue mode, this does result in two "started" messages for every execution. This is why I suggested changing the `WorkflowRunner` log to `.debug` and using "registered" instead of "started". 

Differentiating them this way solves two issues:
1. **Redundancy:** It removes the duplicate `.info` noise in queue mode.
2. **Accuracy:** Since `WorkflowRunner` logs *before* the `credentialsPermissionChecker` runs, calling it "registered" is more accurate. If the check fails, the execution never actually starts, but it was indeed registered. 

In regular (non-queue) mode, `JobProcessor` is never called, so the `.debug` log in `WorkflowRunner` would be the only record of the execution beginning on the Main process.

body:
<!-- metadata:{"confidence":8} -->
P2: `cipher.decrypt(data)` is outside the try-catch block. If stored credential data is corrupted or was encrypted with a different key, the crypto layer will throw an opaque `ERR_OSSL_BAD_DECRYPT` instead of the intended `CredentialResolverDataNotFoundError`. Move the decrypt call inside the try-catch so callers get a consistent error.

<details>

If stored credential data is corrupted or was encrypted with a different key, the crypto layer will throw an opaque `ERR_OSSL_BAD_DECRYPT` instead of the intended `CredentialResolverDataNotFoundError`. Move the decrypt call inside the try-catch so callers get a consistent error.</comment>

body:
What it does: Adds a UNSAFE_PROPERTY_NAMES blocklist (20 entries matching the host-side utils.ts) to findExtendedFunction() in extend.ts. When a property name like constructor, __proto__, or prototype is passed to extend() or extendOptional(), it throws ExpressionExtensionError("Cannot access X due to security concerns") instead of returning
  undefined.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                
                                                                  
  Why: The current (non-VM) engine blocks these via the host-side sanitizePropertyName() check. The VM isolate inherently prevents RCE from these attacks (no host access), but the tests expect the same explicit throw behavior. This also provides defense-in-depth.                                                                                     
                                                                  
  Tests fixed (20):                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         
  - should block extend() constructor access on arrow functions (x2 — extend + extendOptional)
  - should block extend() constructor access on extend itself                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               
  - should block extend() constructor access on extendOptional    
  - should block extend() constructor access on isNaN                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       
  - should block extend() constructor access on parseFloat        
  - should block extend() __proto__ access                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
  - should block extend() prototype access                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
  - should block extend() with custom toString() returning constructor                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
  - should block extend() with custom toString() returning __proto__                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        
  - All of the above duplicated a second time (lines 634-748 duplicate lines 506-690)
author: despairblue

body:
What it does: Replaces globalThis.__data.__sanitize = __sanitize (a plain property assignment) with Object.defineProperty using a getter that returns __sanitize and a setter that throws ExpressionError('Cannot override "__sanitize" due to security concerns').                                                                                       
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  Why: The current engine's __sanitize can't be overridden because tournament evaluates expressions in a sandboxed scope. In the VM, __data is a plain object — a direct assignment is writable by default, so an expression like this.__sanitize = () => "constructor" could bypass the sanitizer. The getter/setter makes the property non-writable and   
  throws visibly on override attempts.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  Test fixed (1):                                                 
  - should block __sanitize override attempt
author: despairblue

body:
What it does: Two changes:
  - Isolate side (reset.ts): Adds $item(itemIndex) function on __data that returns { $json: createDeepLazyProxy(['$item', index, '$json']), $binary: createDeepLazyProxy(['$item', index, '$binary']) }                                                                                                                                                     
  - Bridge side (isolated-vm-bridge.ts): getValueAtPath and getArrayElement callbacks special-case paths starting with ['$item', index] — they call data.$item(index) on the host-side WorkflowDataProxy to get the sub-proxy for that item, then continue navigating the remaining path segments                                                           
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  Why: $item() is legacy syntax (undocumented) that the current engine supports via WorkflowDataProxy. The VM had no implementation — tournament transforms $item(0).$json["key"] to ("$item" in this ? this : global).$item(0).$json["key"], which needs a callable $item on __data.                                                                       
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  Test fixed (1):                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
  - ={{$item(0).$json["Set URL"].json["base_domain"]}}{{$json["link"]}} — expected "leftright", was getting "right"
author: despairblue

body:
What it does: Adds a has trap to the array proxy in lazy-proxy.ts that returns true for numeric indices within 0..length-1.
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  Why: Array.prototype.concat uses the in operator (index in array) to check whether elements exist before copying them. Without a has trap, the proxy's backing array is [] (empty), so 0 in proxy returns false — concat treats every slot as a hole and produces [undefined, undefined, undefined].                                                      
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
  Test fixed (1):                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
  - ={{ [].concat($json["Create or update"].json["vid"]) }} — expected [1, 2, 3], was getting [undefined, undefined, undefined]

But that might not always be the case especially if we introduce custom system roles. I think it might be worth updating this
author: ireneea

body: Could this be the logic inside the `SourceControlContext` constructor? This would also mean moving all the custom project fetching inside the `SourceControlContext` as well. I think `fetchAllProjects`,  `fetchAuthorizedProjects` and `fetchAccessibleWorkflowIds` are only used to create this context.
author: ireneea

body: I can see why this is a massive plus for the speed. Really wonder how this translate with large data volume on memory usage 😅
author: ireneea

body: I did this initially - but then I thought "context" should just be a "thing" that gets passed around rather than needing "service" level DI things and the second best place to put it seemed to be the service 😆
author: geemanjs

body: I don't even think it's a nit, if we change `StatusResourceOwner['type']` to be  `'personal' | 'team' | 'user';` this part would return undefined instead of actual `StatusResourceOwner` when the type is `user`, so I think IMO Irénée's suggestion is much safer for future changes to not cause hidden runtime issues
author: alielkhateeb

body:
I think both perspectives are valid 😂 here’s how I reasoned about it and why I reached the suggestion below:

- It feels right to keep `SourceControlContext` “dumb” (as James suggested)  
  - No dependencies, not a service, and lightweight → easier to pass around  

- It doesn’t feel right to have `createContext` inside `SourceControlScopedService` (as Irénée suggested)  
  - That gives it two responsibilities: constructing the context *and* consuming it

I couldn’t even add a TLDR 😂 My suggestions were too long for it, but here we go:

- Create a `SourceControlContextFactory` service to isolate context creation. This would move the `fetch*` private methods there and keep `SourceControlContext` lightweight and easy to pass around.  
- This would also allow `SourceControlScopedService` to become into something more like a `SourceControlScopeFilterService` (or at least could have a better name IMO).  
- [Optionally] We could cache `hasAccessToAllProjects` and move it to the factory when creating the context. That would avoid recomputing `hasGlobalAccess` on every call and would be stored as a simple `readonly hasAccessToAllProjects: boolean` on SourceControlContext class
author: alielkhateeb

body: When testing locally - I found that `master` spiked to `~1GB` of memory - whereas after this PR it spiked to `~750MB` so 🤞 this translates on other instances
author: geemanjs

body: Changing the logic here allowed us to conditionally call `readFoldersFromSourceControlFile` only when context does NOT have access to all projects, which improves performance further
author: alielkhateeb

body: 🔥 🚀 I like how clean this is 😍

body: Could we just hoist the mock<User>() calls to file scope and reuse them? That avoids the Proxy stacking (one layer instead of N) while keeping the type contract in place. My concern with createTestUser + as unknown as is that it becomes the go-to pattern, if the User shape changes, these tests silently pass.

body:
**33 issues found** across 440 files

**Note:** This PR contains a large number of files. cubic only reviews up to 75 files per PR, so some files may not have been reviewed.

<details>
<summary>Prompt for AI agents (unresolved issues)</summary>

```text

Check if these issues are valid — if so, understand the root cause of each and fix them. If appropriate, use sub-agents to investigate and fix each issue separately.


<file name="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/cli.ts">

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/cli.ts:7">
P1: Custom agent: **Quality & Performance Review**

Avoid eager-loading `GatewayClient` at CLI startup; this pulls native tool dependencies into all invocations and can fail on hosts without those binaries. Load it lazily inside `main()` instead.</violation>
</file>

<file name="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/tools/monitor-utils.ts">

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/tools/monitor-utils.ts:1">
P1: Custom agent: **Quality & Performance Review**

Avoid eager top-level import of the native node-screenshots module. Lazy-load it at the point of use so processes that don’t need screenshots don’t fail on startup.</violation>
</file>

<file name="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/tools/screenshot/index.ts">

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/tools/screenshot/index.ts:1">
P1: Custom agent: **Quality & Performance Review**

Avoid eager top-level import of `node-screenshots`; load it lazily inside `isSupported()` to prevent unnecessary native module loading in every process.</violation>
</file>

<file name="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/tools/mouse-keyboard/mouse-keyboard.ts">

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/tools/mouse-keyboard/mouse-keyboard.ts:1">
P1: Custom agent: **Quality & Performance Review**

Eager top-level import of native module `@jitsi/robotjs`. This is a compiled C++ addon — if the binary is missing or incompatible, the entire `fs-proxy` gateway crashes at startup, taking down all tool categories (filesystem, shell, browser, etc.) that don't depend on it. Use a lazy `await import('@jitsi/robotjs')` inside each `execute` method so the failure is scoped to mouse/keyboard tools only.</violation>
</file>

<file name="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/gateway-client.ts">

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/gateway-client.ts:18">
P1: Custom agent: **Quality & Performance Review**

Eager top-level imports of `ScreenshotModule` and `MouseKeyboardModule` transitively load native binaries (`node-screenshots` and `@jitsi/robotjs`) at module load time. If the native binaries are absent, the process crashes before `isSupported()` can gracefully disable these modules.

Lazy-import them at point of use inside `getAllDefinitions()`, matching the `BrowserModule` pattern already in this file.</violation>

<violation number="2" location="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/gateway-client.ts:236">
P1: Custom agent: **Security Review**

API key/session key is exposed in the SSE URL query parameter. URL query strings are routinely logged by reverse proxies, load balancers, CDNs, and server access logs, making the credential easily extractable from log stores.

The `eventsource` v3 package supports a custom `fetch` option — pass the key via the `X-Gateway-Key` header (consistent with the other endpoints in this file) instead of the query string.</violation>

<violation number="3" location="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/gateway-client.ts:266">
P1: HTTP 500 is misclassified as an auth error. Transient server errors (deploys, crashes, OOMs) will increment `authRetryCount` and permanently kill the client after 5 attempts via `onPersistentFailure`, even though the server would eventually recover. Only 401/403 should trigger re-initialization; 500s should fall through to simple SSE reconnection.</violation>
</file>

<file name="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/tools/screenshot/screenshot.ts">

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/tools/screenshot/screenshot.ts:1">
P1: Custom agent: **Quality & Performance Review**

Avoid eager top-level import of the native `sharp` module; load it lazily at the point of use to prevent startup crashes/memory overhead when the screenshot path isn’t used.</violation>

<violation number="2" location="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/tools/screenshot/screenshot.ts:83">
P1: Clamp region coordinates to monitor bounds and guard against non-positive crop dimensions before calling `crop()`, otherwise off-screen inputs can trigger runtime errors.</violation>
</file>

<file name="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/daemon.ts">

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/daemon.ts:63">
P0: Custom agent: **Security Review**

Wildcard `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` on a localhost daemon that controls shell, filesystem, and input devices allows any website to interact with it (disconnect sessions, read status, attempt connections). This is a well-known localhost service attack vector.

Use the already-available `config.allowedOrigins` (and/or the currently-connected `state.connectedUrl` origin) to set a restrictive `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` instead of `*`. If no origins are configured, deny cross-origin requests entirely or require a per-request token.</violation>

<violation number="2" location="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/daemon.ts:130">
P0: `allowedOrigins` validation uses `startsWith`, which allows origin-spoofing and can bypass the user confirmation step.</violation>

<violation number="3" location="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/daemon.ts:150">
P2: Persistent connection failures clear daemon state but never call `onStatusChange('disconnected')`, leaving external status indicators out of sync.</violation>
</file>

<file name="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/logger.ts">

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/logger.ts:69">
P2: Use async file append instead of `appendFileSync` to avoid blocking the event loop during logging.

(Based on your team's feedback about avoiding synchronous filesystem APIs in Node services.) [FEEDBACK_USED]</violation>
</file>

<file name="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/package.json">

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/package.json:31">
P2: `module` points to a non-published TypeScript source file. Point it to a built artifact (or remove it) to avoid broken package resolution in consumers.</violation>
</file>

<file name="packages/@n8n/instance-ai/src/agent/instance-agent.ts">

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/instance-ai/src/agent/instance-agent.ts:113">
P2: Empty if-block with no `return` means `Mastra` is recreated on every call, defeating the cache. The comment says "Only recreate Mastra if the storage instance changed", but this code unconditionally falls through to the constructor. If reuse is intended when the storage key matches, add `return;` inside the if-block. If recreation is always required (because the constructor is the only way to register a new agent), remove the dead if-block and the `cachedMastra`/`cachedMastraStorageKey` variables to avoid misleading future readers.</violation>
</file>

<file name="packages/@n8n/instance-ai/eslint.config.mjs">

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/instance-ai/eslint.config.mjs:8">
P2: This override unintentionally disables most naming-convention checks for the package by replacing the base rule options instead of adding a narrow exception.</violation>
</file>

<file name="packages/@n8n/instance-ai/src/tools/best-practices/guides/data-extraction.ts">

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/instance-ai/src/tools/best-practices/guides/data-extraction.ts:65">
P3: Remove the stray `]` in the Split Out guidance sentence to avoid malformed documentation text.</violation>
</file>

<file name="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/tools/browser/index.ts">

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/tools/browser/index.ts:61">
P1: Do not catch all errors as "browser module not supported". This currently hides real initialization/config failures and downgrades them into a silent fallback. Only return `null` for missing `@n8n/mcp-browser`; rethrow other errors.

(Based on your team's feedback about reducing non-critical log noise and using appropriate log levels.) [FEEDBACK_USED]</violation>
</file>

<file name="packages/@n8n/instance-ai/docs/sandboxing.md">

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/instance-ai/docs/sandboxing.md:157">
P2: This statement is inaccurate: local sandboxing is not fully blocked in production for the per-builder path.</violation>
</file>

<file name="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/tools/shell/shell-execute.ts">

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/tools/shell/shell-execute.ts:29">
P1: Missing `child.on('error')` handler. If `spawn` fails to start the process (e.g., shell binary not found, permission error), the unhandled `'error'` event on the `ChildProcess` EventEmitter will throw an uncaught exception and crash the host process. Additionally, the `'close'` event won't fire in this scenario, so the promise hangs until timeout.

Add an error listener that clears the timer and resolves with an error result.</violation>
</file>

<file name="packages/@n8n/api-types/src/schemas/agent-run-reducer.ts">

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/api-types/src/schemas/agent-run-reducer.ts:161">

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/tools/filesystem/fs-utils.ts:133">

P1: Missing `child.on('error')` handler. If `spawn` fails to start the process (e.g., shell binary not found, permission error), the unhandled `'error'` event on the `ChildProcess` EventEmitter will throw an uncaught exception and crash the host process. Additionally, the `'close'` event won't fire in this scenario, so the promise hangs until timeout.

Add an error listener that clears the timer and resolves with an error result.

<violation number="1" location="packages/@n8n/fs-proxy/src/tools/shell/shell-execute.ts:1">
P1: Custom agent: **Quality & Performance Review**

Eager top-level imports of `@anthropic-ai/sandbox-runtime` and `@vscode/ripgrep` are only used in the macOS code path (`if (isMac)`) but are loaded on every platform. Native modules that ship platform-specific binaries can crash at import time when the binary is missing, and they waste memory on platforms where they are never used.

**Fix:** Use dynamic `await import()` inside the `if (isMac)` block in `spawnCommand` instead of top-level imports.</violation>

body:
P1: Custom agent: **Quality & Performance Review** Eager top-level imports of `@anthropic-ai/sandbox-runtime` and `@vscode/ripgrep` are only used in the macOS code path (`if (isMac)`) but are loaded on every platform. Native modules that ship platform-specific binaries can crash at import time when the binary is missing, and they waste memory on platforms where they are never used. **Fix:** Use dynamic `await import()` inside the `if (isMac)` block in `spawnCommand` instead of top-level imports.
Prompt for AI agents ```text Check if this issue is valid — if so, understand the root cause and fix it. ```suggestion
			void this.memoryService
				.checkThreadOwnership(userId, threadId)
				.then((currentOwnership) => {
					if (currentOwnership === 'other_user') {
						pendingEvents.length = 0;
						res.end();
						return;
					}
					ownershipVerified = true;
					for (const buffered of pendingEvents) {
						this.writeSseEvent(res, buffered);
					}
					pendingEvents.length = 0;
				})
				.catch(() => {
					pendingEvents.length = 0;
					res.end();
				})
				.finally(() => {
					ownershipCheckInFlight = false;
				});
```

body:
What happens if this signal never reaches the worker? E.g. if worker has crashed or due to network issues. What happens to the job, will it still be cancelled?

Also where does the execution now get moved to canceled state?
author: tomi

body:
> What happens if this signal never reaches the worker? E.g. if worker has crashed or due to network issues. What happens to the job, will it still be cancelled?

Signal delivery failure is a limitation of Redis pubsub being fire and forget. This PR [scopes out that failure mode](https://linear.app/n8n/issue/CAT-2616/stopped-executions-continue-running-on-workers-in-queue-mode#comment-71393165) but, if the worker is alive but signal is lost, the job finishes normally, whereas if the worker crashed even though the signal was sent correctly, the job is marked as failed.

> Also where does the execution now get moved to canceled state?

 In thw worker, `workflowRun` resolves with `run.status === 'canceled'` so the worker throws `ManualExecutionCancelledError`. Bull catches that error from the processor callback and moves the job to failed state, i.e. Bull's own lifecycle handles the Redis key cleanup.
author: ivov

    participant UI as Frontend (Vue)

    Bus-->>UI: Update statusMessage (pulsing dot)

    ICS->>DB: listMessages(threadId)

        ICS->>ICS: Extract user/assistant text (Skip tools/binary)

        alt LLM Success
            LLM_S-->>ICS: Structured Summary (5 sections)
            ICS->>DB: NEW: updateThread metadata (summary + lastMessageId)
        else LLM Failure
            LLM_S-->>ICS: Error (Timeout/API error)
            ICS->>ICS: Log warning (Proceed without summary)
        end
    else Below Threshold / Small Delta
        ICS->>DB: getCachedSummary()
        DB-->>ICS: Existing summary (if any)
    end

    ICS-->>IAS: Return <conversation-summary> XML block
    
    IAS->>Bus: NEW: publish("status", "") 
    Bus-->>UI: Clear status indicator

    IAS->>IAS: CHANGED: Prepend summary to user message
    
    IAS->>LLM_O: agent.stream(summary + backgroundTasks + prompt)
    
    loop Streaming
        LLM_O-->>UI: text-delta events
    end
```
